note:
足球传奇人物马拉多纳是有史以来最伟大的球员之一，享年60岁。
 这位前阿根廷进攻中场和主教练在他的布宜诺斯艾利斯家中心脏病发作。
 他于11月初在脑血栓上成功手术，将因酒精依赖而接受治疗。
 马拉多纳是阿根廷队赢得1986年世界杯冠军时的队长，他在八分之一决赛中对英格兰队打进了著名的“上帝之手”进球。
  在社交媒体上的声明中，阿根廷足协表达了“对我们传奇之死的最深切的哀悼”，并补充说：“您将永远在我们心中。”
 阿根廷总统阿尔贝托·费尔南德斯（Alberto Fernandez）宣布了为期三天的全国哀悼，他说：“您带我们到了世界之巅。您使我们非常高兴。你们是所有人中最伟大的。
  马拉多纳在俱乐部生涯中曾为巴塞罗那和那不勒斯效力，赢得过意大利方面的两个意甲冠军。
他在阿根廷的91场比赛中打进34球，代表他们参加了四届世界杯。
 马拉多纳带领他的国家参加了1990年的意大利决赛，在那里他们被西德击败，然后在1994年再次成为美国的队长，但在一次麻黄素药物测试失败后被送回家。
 在职业生涯的后半段，马拉多纳因可卡因成瘾而苦苦挣扎，并在1991年对该药呈阳性反应后被禁赛15个月。
 他在阿根廷巨人博卡青年队（Boca Juniors）的第二个任职期间，于1997年37岁生日退役。
 马拉多纳在职业生涯中曾短暂在阿根廷担任过两支球队，他在2008年被任命为国家队的主教练，并在2010年世界杯足球赛后离开，后者在八强赛中被德国队击败。
 随后，他管理着阿拉伯联合酋长国和墨西哥的车队，并在去世时负责阿根廷的顶级飞行比赛中的吉姆妮西娅·埃斯格里玛。
   巴西传奇人物贝利（Pele）致敬马拉多纳（Maradona），在推特上写道：“真是个不幸的消息。我失去了一个好朋友，世界也失去了一个传奇。还有很多话要说，但就目前而言，愿上帝赐予家庭成员力量。那天，我希望我们可以在空中一起踢球。”
前英格兰前锋和今日比赛的主持人加里·莱恩克（Gary Lineker）曾是1986年世界杯在阿根廷队击败的英格兰队的成员，他说马拉多纳“在一定距离上，是我们这一代中最好的球员，可以说是有史以来最伟大的球员” 。
 前热刺和阿根廷中场球员奥西·阿迪莱斯（Ossie Ardiles）说：“感谢亲爱的迪吉托，感谢您的友谊，为足球提供了崇高的敬意。是最好的。RIP我亲爱的朋友。”
 马拉卡纳（Boad Juniors）在马拉多纳（Maradona）享受了两次魔术并结束了他的职业生涯，对前任球员表示“永恒的感谢”。
  巴黎圣日耳曼队和巴西前锋内马尔发布了他与马拉多纳年轻时的照片，称阿根廷为“足球传奇”。
     巴塞罗那是马拉多纳效力的阿根廷以外的第一家具乐部。在1982年至1984年之间，他共36次出场打进22球。
  马拉多纳的另一个前俱乐部那不勒斯致敬。他在1984年至1991年之间为俱乐部效力，出场188次。
note_en:
Football legend Diego Maradona, one of the greatest players of all time, has died at the age of 60.
 The former Argentina attacking midfielder and manager suffered a heart attack at his Buenos Aires home.
 He had successful surgery on a brain blood clot earlier in November and was to be treated for alcohol dependency.
 Maradona was captain when Argentina won the 1986 World Cup, scoring the famous 'Hand of God' goal against England in the quarter-finals.
  In a statement on social media, the Argentine Football Association expressed "its deepest sorrow for the death of our legend", adding: "You will always be in our hearts."
 Declaring three days of national mourning, Alberto Fernandez, the president of Argentina, said: "You took us to the top of the world. You made us immensely happy. You were the greatest of them all.
  Maradona played for Barcelona and Napoli during his club career, winning two Serie A titles with the Italian side.
 He scored 34 goals in 91 appearances for Argentina, representing them in four World Cups.
 Maradona led his country to the 1990 final in Italy, where they were beaten by West Germany, before captaining them again in the United States in 1994, but was sent home after failing a drugs test for ephedrine.
 During the second half of his career, Maradona struggled with cocaine addiction and was banned for 15 months after testing positive for the drug in 1991.
 He retired from professional football in 1997, on his 37th birthday, during his second stint at Argentine giants Boca Juniors.
 Having briefly managed two sides in Argentina during his playing career, Maradona was appointed head coach of the national team in 2008 and left after the 2010 World Cup, where his side were beaten by Germany in the quarter-finals.
 He subsequently managed teams in the United Arab Emirates and Mexico and was in charge of Gimnasia y Esgrima in Argentina's top flight at the time of his death.
   Brazil legend  Pele  led tributes to Maradona, writing on Twitter: "What sad news. I lost a great friend and the world lost a legend. There is still much to be said, but for now, may God give strength to family members. One day, I hope we can play ball together in the sky."
 Former England striker and Match of the Day host  Gary Lineker,  who was part of the England team beaten by Argentina at the 1986 World Cup, said Maradona was "by some distance, the best player of my generation and arguably the greatest of all time".
 Ex-Tottenham and Argentina midfielder  Ossie Ardiles  said: "Thank dear Dieguito for your friendship, for your football, sublime, without comparison. Simply, the best football player in the history of football. So many enjoyable moments together. Impossible to say which one was the best. RIP my dear friend."
 Boca Juniors, where Maradona enjoyed two spells and finished his career, gave "eternal thanks" to their former player.
  Paris St-Germain and Brazil forward Neymar posted a photo of him as a youngster with Maradona, calling the Argentine a "legend of football".
     Barcelona was the first club outside of Argentina that Maradona played for. He scored 22 goals in 36 appearances between 1982 and 1984.
  Another of Maradona's former clubs, Napoli, paid tribute. He played for the club between 1984 and 1991, making 188 appearances.
title: 迭戈·马拉多纳去世

note:
2016年，联邦通讯委员会举行了一次非常特别的拍卖。几十年来，许多电磁频谱（电视，无线电和无线信号在其上传播的无线电波）归电视台所有。但是近年来，手机和其他无线技术一直渴望更多频谱。
  因此，FCC计划拍卖掉很大一部分电磁频谱。结果将为未来更好的无线服务铺平道路。拍卖非常复杂，将涉及数年的计划，巨额资金，当然还有……人们正在尝试使用该系统。
  今天在节目中，讲述了有关拍卖设计，当地电视台，华尔街以及数量惊人的红色甘草和Tums的故事。
  澄清：一家私募股权公司以略高于700万美元的价格购买了WBGN网络中的所有11个站点，而不仅仅是旗舰WBGN站点。该公司最终以2010万美元的价格将其出售。该公司总共出售了五个加油站，总价为7390万美元。音频已更新以反映这一点。
          我们并不总是参加拍卖，但我们正在竞标您的注意力：订阅我们的时事通讯。
note_en:
In 2016, the Federal Communications Commission held a very special auction. For decades, a lot of the electromagnetic spectrum — the airwaves over which TV, radio and wireless signals are transmitted — were owned by TV stations. But in recent years, cell phones and other wireless technologies have been hungry for more of the spectrum.
  So the FCC planned to auction off a big part of the electromagnetic spectrum. The result would pave the way for a future of even better wireless service. The auction was incredibly complicated and would involve years of planning, enormous sums of money and of course... people trying to game the system.
  Today on the show, a story about auction design, local TV, Wall Street, and a staggering amount of red licorice and Tums.
  Clarification: A private equity firm purchased all eleven stations in the WBGN network, not just the flagship WBGN station, for just over $7 million. The flagship station was sold into the auction by the firm for $20.1 million. In total the firm sold five stations for $73.9 million. The audio has been updated to reflect this.
          We don't always participate in auctions, but we're putting in a bid for your attention: subscribe to our   Newsletter .
title: 破解完美拍卖

note:
流行病放大了长期存在的说法，即重新技能是失业和向上流动的答案。但是，实际上这是解决劳动力困境的灵丹妙药吗？
 当伦敦在三月份陷入封锁时，巴尔塔萨·罗梅罗（Baltasar Romero）很快意识到自己作为预订经理的工作可能会遇到麻烦。他管理的团队从10人缩减为1人，他的工作时间减少了三分之一。
 这位34岁的老人说：“很显然，在接下来的几年中，待客工作将非常困难。” “八月份，他们使我的职位多余。因此，它们使我很容易跳进了根本性的变革。”
 9月，他开始了为期三个月的全日制软件工程课程。他说，改变卡已有一段时间了，大流行只是他需要的推动力。罗梅罗补充说，他对技术的热爱似乎使他的职业生涯非常适合，但是行业内经常要求更多编码员的电话无疑有助于他做出决定。
 他说：“似乎每个人迟早都要跳入这种环境。”
 由于大流行已经使数百万人失业，并加速了整个行业的重塑，全世界的工人都在看到信号，即新技能将成为“新常态”中稳定就业的关键。例如，9月，英国首相鲍里斯·约翰逊（Boris Johnson）宣布了旨在提高工人技能的计划，形式包括免费的大学课程，数字技能训练营和更多的学徒计划，以“更好地从冠状病毒中重建”。
  但是专家说，如果最后没有足够的工作，对数百万人进行新技术技能的培训是没有意义的。俄勒冈大学的政治经济学家戈登·拉弗尔（Gordon Lafer）说：“这可能对个人有用，但不能作为经济政策起作用。” “每次遇到经济问题，工作培训都会被淘汰……而且从来没有奏效。”
42岁的利兹克莱尔·温特伯顿（Claire Winterbottom）来自利兹，开始了她的重新杀戮之旅，疫情仍在几个月之后。她已从债务顾问的工作中休养，并决定花时间重新培训一名Web开发人员。她一直对技术感兴趣，但也热衷于“防未来”。
 一月份，她在针对女性的为期四个月的免费课程中获得了一席之地。她被告知，除前两个队列外，其他所有人均已获得技术工作。但是，今年的课程于4月完成，当时是大流行的第一波高峰，而近七个月后，她的16个班级中只有四个在该行业找到了工作。温特伯顿表示，随着经济衰退的到来，以及对新开发商的需求逐渐枯竭，这不足为奇。 “您还与其他遭受流行病困扰的人学习如何编码。因此，更多的人拥有更多的技能，但是工作更少。”她补充道。
 拉弗（Lafer）说，自1980年代以来就一直在研究美国的职业培训计划，这种认为不稳定的职位工人现在可以通过培训解决的想法特别不合逻辑。人们之所以失业，是因为这种大流行已经使大量的经济蒙上了阴影，而不是因为缺乏技能。他说：“如果要进行职业培训就不是现在了。”
  但是，即使在大流行之前，重新杀戮的故事仍处于不稳定状态。随着全球化和技术进步的双重力量侵蚀了高薪的蓝领工作和日常办公室工作，各国政府定期将其作为解决方案进行推广。逻辑是，随着制造业等行业的衰落以及IT繁荣等行业的发展，雇主和工人所需的技能之间的差距越来越大，尤其是在STEM（科学，技术，工程和数学）相关领域。因此，向工人传授诸如编码之类的技能，可以用一块石头杀死两只鸟：在满足行业需求的同时，帮助流离失所的工人改善职业生涯。
 但是证据并不能支持“技能差距”的说法。
 美国劳工部前首席经济学家海迪·希尔霍尔兹（Heidi Shierholz）指出，出现此类问题的最明显信号可能是失业率低，工资增长强劲的职业，这反映了对具有需求技能的人的竞争。但是一直缺乏这种证据。在2018年，只有法律领域的职业接近。她说，实际上，雇主报告招聘困难，因为他们提供的工资与他们所追求的技能不匹配。
  罗格斯大学社会学家哈尔·萨尔兹曼说，如果有的话，数字表明技能供过于求。他和同事在2018年的一篇论文中显示，只有大约60％至70％的美国计算机和工程专业毕业生在STEM工作，而那些研究生命科学，物理科学和数学的人下降到10％至5​​0％。去年国家统计局发布的一项研究在英国记录了类似的证据，即技能短缺不是问题所在。数据显示，2017年有16％的工人因工作而受过高等教育，而毕业生的这一比例升至31％。
Lafer说，问题的一部分是通常鼓励人们对职业进行再培训，以代表全部工作的一小部分，而且在许多地方，技术被用来使任务自动化，从而消除了对受过培训的员工的需求。
 那么，为什么政策制定者和企业领导者仍会继续专注于技能提升？ Lafer认为，这为他们提供了一条轻松的出路，因为他们为工人的黯淡就业前景指责是他们未能获得正确的技能，而不是面对经济的更深层次的结构变化。他说，更好的选择可能是推动其他有需求的行业的更高工资和条件的推动，这些行业雇佣了很多人但是却没有得到应有的回报，例如建筑，医疗保健和教育。但他承认，这场辩论是有争议的。
  对于布鲁金斯学会（Brookings Institution）的马塞拉（Marcela Escobari）而言，对再技能的关注与其说是针对工人的“阴谋”，不如说是激励措施错位和对银弹的过度渴望。去年12月，她和同事发表了一份名为《关于再技能的现实主义》的报告，该报告得出结论，近视对技能的关注掩盖了许多其他可能使工人陷入死胡同的障碍。
 “肯定有技能很重要的地方。她说：“但是，还有许多其他原因导致人们开始从事低薪工作，而这些工作没有流动性。”
 在研究现实世界中的工作转变时，他们发现大多数人不需要再培训。与其说是选择合适的行业，不如说是因为他们现有的技能很匹配，或者是所在领域的需求很高。她和同事们创建了一个在线工具，该工具使用有关工作转移的真实数据来告诉人们成功地转移到其职业和领域的其他角色。他们还希望雇主能看一下结果，以确定应该从哪个行业招聘。
 她说，政策制定者应集中精力简化此类过渡。这可能包括创造可转移的福利，使工人在更换雇主时可以随身携带诸如健康计划和退休计划之类的东西，还可以激励雇主创造更多的入门级职位，并逆转数十年来内部培训计划的资金削减。
  但是她说，还需要付出更多的努力来消除那些真正需要重新技能的人的障碍，他们通常最无能为力。对于低薪工人来说，通常归结为简单的后勤工作：能够抽出时间休息，或者有车去上培训课。而且，由于重新技能计划通常是根据其结果来衡量的，因此他们经常筛选候选人以选择最有可能成功的候选人。
这并不是说重新技能对个人来说是一个普遍的坏主意。大流行期间，现年28岁的Stefanie Lis因在利兹的餐厅工作而休假，并利用这段时间在线学习编码。三个月后，她获得了曼彻斯特网络开发公司Huddle的带薪实习，该公司从那以后变成了一份工作。
 她坚信，当今可用的大量免费或廉价在线资源意味着任何人只要认真考虑就可以学习编码。但是她意识到，休假使她将学习视为全职工作，而且她很幸运地找到了一个愿意接受刚起步的开发人员的雇主。 “他们真的花时间忍耐，给我展示东西并支持我。”
 尽管到目前为止她一直在挣扎，但温特伯顿也对她最终会找到一名开发人员充满信心。但是她说，如果这样做的话，这将取决于她对编码的热情，更不用说她已经受雇并且不必担心账单堆积的事实。她补充说，这对每个人都没有意义，而且她认为对于政客们来说这是轻率的，暗示这是大流行对就业市场造成的破坏的答案。
 “您必须非常努力地工作，并进行所有这些额外的活动，并使自己完全沉浸其中。没有那种热情，我看不到他们走到哪里。”
note_en:
The pandemic has amplified the long-enduring narrative that reskilling is the answer to unemployment and upward mobility. But is it actually a panacea for workforce woes?
 When London went into lockdown in March, Baltasar Romero quickly realised his job as a reservations manager might be in trouble. The team he was managing was cut from 10 people to just one, and his hours were reduced by a third.
 “It was clear that working in hospitality was going to be quite difficult for the next few years,” says the 34-year-old. “In August, they made my position redundant. So they made it very easy for me to just jump into a radical change.”
 In September, he started a three-month, full-time software-engineering course. He says a change had been in the cards for some time, and the pandemic was just the push he needed. Romero adds that his affinity for technology seemed to make the career a good fit, but the regular calls from the industry for more coders certainly helped make his decision.
 “It seems that sooner or later everybody should jump into this environment,” he says.
 As the pandemic has put millions out of work and is hastening the reshaping of entire industries, workers around the world are seeing signals that new skills will be the key to steady employment in the 'new normal'. For example, in September, UK Prime Minister Boris Johnson  announced plans aimed at upskilling workers in the form of free college courses, digital-skills bootcamps and more apprenticeships to “build back better from coronavirus”.
  But experts say there’s little point training millions of people in new technical skills if there aren’t enough jobs at the end of it. “It could work for an individual, but it doesn't work as an economic policy,” says Gordon Lafer, a political economist at the University of Oregon. “Every time there's an economic problem job training is trotted out… and it's never worked."
 The pandemic was still a few months away when 42-year-old Claire Winterbottom, from Leeds, started her reskilling journey. She had taken adoption leave from her job as a debt advisor, and decided to use the time to retrain as a web developer. She’d always been interested in technology, but was also keen to “future-proof” herself.
 In January, she secured a place on a free four-month course aimed at women. She was told all but two of the previous cohort had secured technology jobs. But this year’s course finished in April, at the peak of the pandemic’s first wave, and nearly seven months later only four of her class of 16 have found work in the industry. Winterbottom says it’s hardly surprising as the recession bites, and demand for new developers dries up. “You're also up against other people who've taken the pandemic to learn how to code. So, there’s more people with more skills, but there's less jobs,” she adds.
 The idea that the precarious position workers find themselves in today could be solved by training is particularly illogical, says Lafer, who has been studying job-training schemes in the US since the 1980s. People are out of work because the pandemic has shuttered huge swathes of the economy, not because of a lack of skills. “If job training was ever going to work it's not now,” he says.
  But even before the pandemic, the narrative of reskilling was on shaky ground. As the twin forces of globalisation and technological progress have eroded well-paid blue-collar jobs and routine office work, governments have regularly promoted it as the solution. The logic is that as industries like manufacturing decline and others like IT boom, there’s a growing gap between the skills employers need and those of workers, particularly in STEM (science, technology, engineering and maths) related fields. Teaching workers in-demand skills, such as coding, could therefore kill two birds with one stone: meeting industry needs while helping displaced workers revamp their careers.
 But the evidence doesn’t back up the assertion of a ‘skills gap’.
 Former US Department of Labor chief economist Heidi Shierholz has  pointed out that the clearest signal of such a problem would be occupations with a combination of low unemployment and strong wage growth, reflecting competition for those with in-demand skills. But this evidence is consistently lacking; in 2018, only occupations in the legal field came close. In reality, employers report difficulty hiring because the wages they offer don’t match the skills they’re after, she says.
  If anything, the numbers point to an oversupply of skills, says Hal Salzman, a sociologist at Rutgers University.  In a 2018 paper, he and colleagues showed that only about 60 to 70% of US computing and engineering graduates land jobs in STEM, dropping to between 10 and 50% for those studying life sciences, physical sciences and maths. A  study from the Office for National Statistics published last year recorded similar evidence in the UK that a shortage of skills is not the issue. The data showed that 16% of workers in 2017 were overeducated for their jobs, which rose to 31% for graduates.
 Lafer says that part of the problem is that the occupations people are typically encouraged to retrain for represent a small fraction of total jobs, and that in many places, technology is being used to automate tasks to eliminate the need for trained employees.
 Why, then, do policymakers and business leaders continue to focus on reskilling? Lafer thinks it gives them an easy way out by laying the blame for workers’ dim employment prospects on their failure to acquire the right skills rather than facing up to deeper structural changes in the economy. He says that a better option might be a push for better pay and conditions in other in demand industries that employ lots of people but are poorly rewarded such as construction, healthcare and education. But that debate is contentious, he admits.
  For Marcela Escobari at the Brookings Institution, the focus on reskilling is less a ‘conspiracy’ against workers than a tangle of misaligned incentives and over-eagerness for a silver bullet. Last December, she and colleagues published a report called  Realism About Reskilling, which concluded that a myopic focus on skills obscures the numerous other barriers that can trap workers in dead-end jobs .
 “There's definitely places where skills matter. But there's a lot of other reasons why people are churning through low-wage jobs that don't see any mobility,” she says.
 When they studied real-world job transitions, they found most didn’t require retraining. It was more about picking the right industry to move into, either because their existing skills were a good match or demand was high in their area. She and colleagues created an  online tool that uses real-world data on job transitions to tell people what roles others in their occupation and area have successfully moved to. They also hope employers will look at the results to work out what industries they should be hiring from.
 She says policymakers should focus on making these kinds of transitions easier. That could include creating portable benefits that allow workers to take things like health plans and retirement plans with them when they switch employers, but also incentivising employers to create more entry level positions and reverse decades of funding cuts to internal training schemes.
  But she says there also needs to be more effort to remove barriers for those in genuine need of reskilling who are typically the least able to access it. For low-wage workers, it often comes down to simple logistics: being able to take time off work, or having a car to get to training classes. And because reskilling programs are often measured on their outcomes, they frequently screen candidates to select those most likely to succeed.
 That’s not to say reskilling is a universally bad idea for individuals. Stefanie Lis, 28, was put on furlough from her restaurant job in Leeds during the pandemic, and used the time to study coding online. Three months later, she secured a paid internship with Manchester web development company Huddle, which has since turned into a job.
 She’s adamant that the huge amount of free or cheap online resources available today means anyone can learn to code if they put their mind to it. But she is conscious that the furlough allowed her to treat learning as a full-time job, and she was lucky to find an employer willing to take on a fledgling developer. “They've just really taken the time to be patient and show me things and support me."
 Despite her struggles so far, Winterbottom is also confident she will eventually find work as a developer. But if she does, she says it’ll be down to her passion for coding, not to mention the fact she’s already employed and doesn’t have to worry about bills piling up. She adds that there’s no way it makes sense for everyone, and she thinks its disingenuous for politicians to suggest that this is the answer to the havoc the pandemic has wrought on the job market.
 “You've got to work so hard at it and do all these extra activities and just immerse yourself fully. Without that passion, I can't see them getting anywhere.”
title: 为什么重新技能不能总是保证您获得新工作
title_en: Why reskilling won’t always guarantee you a new job

note:
在酷暑的夜晚，凌晨2点左右，奥兰多·加纳博士（Orlando Garner）醒来，他小女儿的婴儿床旁边传来一阵轰鸣声。他跳下床，发现妻子加布里埃拉（Gabriela）昏倒了，她的额头因发烧而发烧，当时他和他的儿子奥兰多（Orlando Jr.）遭受了同样的痛苦，然后才三个小时。两天后，这将打击他们的幼女Veronica。
 近五个月后，休斯敦贝勒医学院的重症监护医生加纳医生因去年夏天家人遭受的困扰而感到困扰：他无意中将冠状病毒穿梭在家里，使所有人都感到不适。
 他说：“我感到内gui。” “这是我的工作，我想做的就是谋生。它可能杀了我的孩子，可能杀了我的妻子-所有这些，都是因为我。”
 随着得克萨斯州的病例数再次攀升，加纳博士反复发生噩梦，说他的一个孩子死于科维德。他回到了重症监护室，每周工作80个小时，穿上几层大风衣，包括护目镜，N95呼吸器，防护服和头盔式面罩，迫使他大喊大叫。
 当他接连治疗一名患者时，即使再感染极少发生，他也无法摆脱对第一次使用冠状病毒的回弹的恐惧：“难道这将再次给我带来Covid吗？”
 一线医护人员一直是一个不变的人，医务人员在地面上战胜了冠状病毒的肆虐蔓延，一排排地作战。但是，随着案件和死亡打破每日记录，预示着美国历史上最致命的年份之一，那些以人生使命为生的人正处于集体崩溃的边缘。
 在采访中，超过二十名一线医务人员描述了这种持续不断的压力，这种压力已经成为全国医疗保健危机的流行部分。许多相关的焦虑和抑郁念头激增，以及长期的绝望感和日益加深的疲劳感，部分原因是许多美国人似乎对大流行病失去了耐心，他们的态度更加勇敢。
来自全球的调查表明，在以自杀率很高而闻名的一群专业人士中，抑郁，创伤和倦怠的发生率正在上升。尽管有些人寻求治疗或药物来应对，但另一些人则担心参与这些支持系统可能会破坏他们的记录，并阻止未来的雇主雇用他们。
 亚利桑那州尤马市的急诊医学医师克里夫·吉尔曼（Cleavon Gilman）博士说：“我们在牺牲医疗保健提供者的同时，付出了巨大的代价，我们的健康，我们的家庭的健康。他说，春天。 “但我觉得在纽约死去的2万人无缘无故死了。”
 许多人已经到达水库的底部，几乎没有余力，特别是在没有足够的工具来抵御已经杀死了1000多个人的疾病的情况下。
 哥伦比亚大学儿科头颈外科医生Susannah Hills博士说：“我什至都没有想过今天的样子。” “我想不起来上次有人问我这个问题。”
  对于科罗拉多州的一位老年医师Shannon Tapia博士来说，四月是糟糕的。五月也是如此。她在一个长期护理机构工作，在10天内有22人死亡。她说：“在这个数字之后，我不再计数了。”
 一阵平静在夏天的热浪中袭来。但是在最近几周里，塔皮亚博士目睹了这种病毒的重新流行，引发了突发性暴发，并使成群的养老院居民（大流行病最重的人口之一）丧生。
 塔皮亚博士说：“这比春天差很多，”。 “ Covid现在在科罗拉多州将变得疯狂。”
Tapia博士为长期护理机构努力保持库存足够的防护设备而作证，并谴责缺乏适当的测试。直到11月初，塔皮亚（Tapia）博士定期探访一间房屋进行的诊断测试都花了一个多星期的时间才能得出结果，从而加快了病毒在不知情者中的传播。
 丹佛地区的一些疗养院居民因症状不严重而被迅速送出医院，只是症状迅速恶化并在护理设施中死亡。塔皮亚博士说：“事情发生得如此之快。” “没有时间将它们寄回。”
 11月17日晚上，塔皮亚医生接到了满是病人和恐惧者的疗养院的电话。 4名患者在下午5点之间死亡。她说：“这是我一晚上不得不做的最多的死亡宣告。”
 在大流行之前，疗养院的居民已经被视为医学上被忽视的人群。但是，冠状病毒只会加剧老年患者的令人担忧的护理鸿沟。 Tapia博士为她动turn的无助所困扰。她说：“从系统上来说，这让我感到自己失败了。” “最近八个月差点让我失望。”
 暑假结束时，塔皮亚医生（Tapia）曾短暂考虑过退出药物治疗-但她是11岁儿子利亚姆（Liam）的单亲父母。她说：“我需要医学博士来养育我的孩子。”
  尤马市急诊医学医师吉尔曼（Gilman）博士在大流行初期就做好了准备，依靠他在2004年担任伊拉克医院军官的经历。
 他说：“在军队中，他们训练您进行睡眠剥夺，远足，游行。” “您训练自己的身体，与敌人作战。我每天开始跑步，以防万一我感染了病毒。我在门口放了一个箱子来放衣服，这样就不会把它散给我的家人了。”
事实证明，当前的危机是一个陌生而强大的敌人，他将随处可见。
 吉尔曼博士的首个冠状病毒之旅始于去年春天高峰时在纽约长老会的居民。他说，他害怕无法打电话给无法与患病亲戚的家人联系的电话，听到“同样尖刻的哭声，每班两到三遍，”他说。他说，数月的混乱，痛苦和痛苦使他“跌落，沮丧，疲惫”。
 他说：“我会流着眼泪回家，然后昏倒。”
  吉尔曼博士在五月取消了他的婚礼。他的六月毕业于Zoom开始。他在一堆箱子旁边的空公寓里庆祝居住的结束。
  几周之内，他，未婚夫，马里贝尔（Maribel），他们的两个女儿和他的岳母搬到亚利桑那州，那里的案件数量才开始激增。吉尔曼博士重新屈服了。
 自从隐居以来，他们度过了几个月的风雨，即使邻居们开始蜂拥而至，并为他们的假期计划而嗡嗡作响，他们都让孩子们脱离了学校，并且拒绝参加聚会的邀请。
 他说，这里有很多亮点。他们今年夏天搬进来的这个家庭的房子很大，有一个游泳池。他们最近收养了一只小狗。在亚利桑那州小镇的偏远地区，偶尔看到行人的路过，沙漠使他们感到高兴。
从春季开始，吉尔曼（Gilman）博士已成为社交媒体的不可或缺的一环。为了记录持续的危机，他开始在自己的网站上发布日记条目。他的Twitter墙上满是帖子，以纪念那些因Covid-19丧生的人们以及过去九个月致力于遏制潮流的卫生工作者。
 吉尔曼博士说，这就是他对混乱的理解。他所争取的不仅是病毒本身，还包括幻灭感和错误信息的蔓延，在这种情况下，戴着口罩和疏散口罩的现象不断出现。他说：“这是一场持续不断的战斗，这是一场永无止境的战争。”
  去年春天，纽约的护士和医生对护理的定量分配变得非常熟悉。没有培训可以使他们为病毒的愤怒及其后果做好准备。他们试图应对的月复一日，日复一日地挣扎。对于某些人来说，大流行的重担将挥之不去。
 西奈山皇后医院急诊室护士Shikha Dass回忆起3月中旬的夜晚，当时她的八名护士组成的小组不得不纠缠每个15名病人，这是典型工作量的两倍或三倍。达斯女士说：“我们不断获取代码，而患者正在死亡。”她说，患者人数迅速超过了可用的呼吸支持机，迫使医生和护士迅速采取分摊护理措施。
 达斯女士说：“我们没有足够的呼吸机。” “我记得在做C.P.R.和肋骨开裂。这些是我们社区的人-太痛苦了。”
  达斯女士因失眠和烦躁不安而摔跤，对着她的丈夫和孩子之以鼻。数十人散布在急诊室婴儿床上的死者异象，在夜晚的几个小时时游过她的头。像Grey的Anatomy这样的医疗电视剧，充满了代码和蜂鸣器的触发声音，变得难以忍受。她无法抹去医院停车场中整齐的三排冷藏拖车的记忆，每辆拖车都装满了太平间无法容纳的尸体。
 一个夜班结束后的一个早晨，达斯女士爬上了红色的迷你库珀，开始了20分钟的车程。她的车驶向熟悉的路线。 2017年电影《最伟大的表演者》中的一首歌流了出来。自大流行开始以来，达斯女士第一次崩溃并开始哭泣。她打电话给正在上班的丈夫。他没有接。最后，她认识了她最好的朋友。
她说：“我告诉她，‘这些人不会成功，这些人将无法生存。” “我们在那里是为了拯救一个人，挽救一条生命，稳定一个人，以便他们得到进一步的管理。我在这里，无法做到这一点。”
 不久之后，她给一位长期的朋友打电话给心理治疗师安迪·林·科恩费尔德（Andi Lyn Kornfeld），她说达斯女士正处于“绝对且完全急性的创伤后应激障碍”的痛苦之中。
 “我认识Shikha已有13年了，” Kornfeld女士说。 “她是我见过的最坚强的女人之一。而且我从没听过她这样的话。”
  喧嚣的夜间欢呼声，热烈的掌声和叮当声在美国和国外的建筑物和医院的窗户上回荡，大流行前线的人们每天晚上7点听到公众的赞赏。
 “没有人鼓掌了，”圣路易斯华盛顿大学的精神病医生杰西卡·戈德（Jessica Gold）博士说。 “他们过去了。”
 曾经是冠状病毒对话的核心部分的卫生工作者已经在许多方面逐渐淡出人们的视线。像亚利桑那州的吉尔曼博士这样的人，由于医院正在考虑如何负担费用，因此削减了他们的薪水。
 许多人因无法挽救的人的死亡行径和冠状病毒的滥杀滥伤而从广告或广告活动中贴上的“英雄”标签上感到内rec。
俄克拉荷马州的精神病医生尼科尔·华盛顿博士说，“英雄”一词唤起了勇敢和超人的力量，但几乎没有移情的余地。被描绘成坚定的救世主时，卫生工作者“没有容忍弱势的空间或权利。”
  塔皮亚博士，科罗拉多老年病学，开始服用九月抗抑郁药的感觉个月后，“一切从生气着急大怒，只是麻木和绝望。”药物改善了她的视力。但她也担心这些决定可能会危害未来的就业。
 许多州医疗委员会仍在询问医生的精神健康诊断或治疗历史，以申请续签许可证，这对许多本来可能寻求专业帮助的医生是不利的。
  站在前线并不能使卫生工作者比其他任何人都更强大或更安全。 “我并不想成为英雄。我不想成为英雄，”吉尔曼博士说。 “我想活着。”
 在寒冷的假期开始之前，社会泡沫在全国范围内迅速膨胀，医护人员在社区边缘担忧，担心他们是传染病的传播者。
 埃默里大学的内科医师Marshall Fleurant博士认为，他的3岁和4岁的孩子已经习惯了从洗衣服到运动鞋穿衣服的习惯。进入他的家。
 弗勒兰特博士说：“我在洗完澡之前没有抚摸孩子，也没跟孩子说话。 “这就是事实。当他走进门时，您不要碰爸爸。”
与家人休假一周令他大吃一惊，那时他可以毫不恐惧地将小孩子抱在怀里。他说：“我认为他们一定认为这很奇怪。”
  随着冠状病毒继续在全国范围内燃烧，人们被困在一个固定的模式中，医生和护士们一直在评估迄今为止所造成的损害，并试图勾勒出更广阔的前景。他们说，就该国目前的轨迹而言，这一预测是黯淡的。
 芝加哥的医师助理吉娜·萨尔茨曼（Jina Saltzman）说，她对美国对这种病毒的松懈方法越来越失望。
 当案件开始上升时，伊利诺伊州迅速对餐馆和企业实行限制，但萨尔茨曼女士居住的印第安纳州的回应却较慢。 11月中旬，当她拿起披萨时，在一家餐馆看到成群的不露面的人感到惊讶。 “这真令人沮丧。我们每天都会来这里工作，以确保公众安全。”她说。 “但是公众并没有试图确保公众安全。”
 从春季开始，吉尔曼博士就目睹了三名同事和一位堂兄死于该病毒。达斯女士失去了一位亲密的家人朋友，在她的照顾下，他在西奈山皇后医院度过了三个星期。当Fleurant博士的姨妈死于Covid时，“我们永远不会埋葬她，永远也不必致敬。这真是惨重的损失。”
 在一个又一个的州中，人们继续淹没医院的病房，那里的走廊经常为临时病房提供临时病床。自从美国大流行以来，已经记录了超过1200万例病例，在过去几个月中，感染的速度在加快。
 内布拉斯加州大学医学中心的护士吉尔·奈伯克（Jill Naiberk）在2020年花费了全部的防护装备而不是全部。每天约两次，当Naiberk女士需要喝水时，她必须完全脱去长袍，然后重新穿上衣服。
否则，“你又热又汗又臭。”她说。 “带着汗水从房间出来流到房间外面并不少见，而且因为潮湿，您需要更换口罩。”
 这是她连续第九个月担任Covid职务。 “我的房间有16张床。她说：“我们很少公开。” “而且当我们有一张敞开的床时，通常是因为有人去世了。”
 她的许多I.C.U.患者年龄在40多岁或50多岁。她说：“他们看着我们，说的是'不要让我死'和'我想我应该戴那个面具'。”
 有时，她在回家的路上哭泣，独自一人与两只狗住在一起。她79岁的母亲仅住在几所房子之外。
  她说：“我会告诉所有人我可以再次安全地拥抱你的时刻，准备好。” “因为我永不放手。”
note_en:
About 2 a.m. on a sweltering summer night, Dr. Orlando Garner awoke to the sound of a thud next to his baby daughter’s crib. He leapt out of bed to find his wife, Gabriela, passed out, her forehead hot with the same fever that had stricken him and his son, Orlando Jr., then 3, just hours before. Two days later, it would hit their infant daughter, Veronica.
 Nearly five months later, Dr. Garner, a critical care physician at the Baylor College of Medicine in Houston, is haunted by what befell his family last summer: He had inadvertently shuttled the coronavirus home, and sickened them all.
 “I felt so guilty,” he said. “This is my job, what I wanted to do for a living. And it could have killed my children, could have killed my wife — all this, because of me.”
 With the case count climbing again in Texas, Dr. Garner has recurring nightmares that one of his children has died from Covid. He’s returned to 80-hour weeks in the intensive care unit, donning layers of pandemic garb including goggles, an N95 respirator, a protective body suit and a helmet-like face shield that forces him to yell to be heard.
 As he treats one patient after another, he can’t shake the fear that his first bout with the coronavirus won’t be his last, even though reinfection is rare: “Is this going to be the one who gives me Covid again?”
 Frontline health care workers have been the one constant, the medical soldiers forming row after row in the ground war against the raging spread of the coronavirus. But as cases and deaths  shatter daily records, foreshadowing one of the deadliest years in American history, the very people whose life mission is caring for others are on the verge of collective collapse.
 In interviews, more than two dozen frontline medical workers described the unrelenting stress that has become an endemic part of the health care crisis nationwide. Many related  spikes in anxiety and depressive thoughts, as well as a  chronic sense of hopelessness and deepening fatigue, spurred in part by the cavalier attitudes of many Americans who seem to have lost patience with the pandemic.
  Surveys from around the globe have recorded  rising rates of  depression,  trauma and  burnout among a group of professionals already known for  high rates of suicide. And while some have sought therapy or medications to cope, others fear that engaging in these support systems could blemish their records and dissuade future employers from hiring them.
 “We’re sacrificing so much as health care providers — our health, our family’s health,” said Dr. Cleavon Gilman, an emergency medicine physician in Yuma, Ariz. “You would think that the country would have learned its lesson” after the spring, he said. “But I feel like the 20,000 people that died in New York died for nothing.”
 Many have reached the bottom of their reservoir, with little left to give, especially without sufficient tools to defend themselves against a disease that  has killed more than 1,000 of them.
 “I haven’t even thought about how I am today,” said Dr. Susannah Hills, a pediatric head and neck surgeon at Columbia University. “I can’t think of the last time somebody asked me that question.”
  For Dr. Shannon Tapia, a geriatrician in Colorado, April was bad. So was May. At one long-term care facility she staffed, 22 people died in 10 days. “After that number, I stopped counting,” she said.
 A bit of a lull coasted in on a wave of summer heat. But in recent weeks, Dr. Tapia has watched the virus resurge, sparking sudden outbreaks and felling nursing home residents —  one of the pandemic’s most hard-hit populations — in droves.
 “This is much, much worse than the spring,” Dr. Tapia said. “Covid is going crazy in Colorado right now.”
 Dr. Tapia bore witness as long-term care facilities struggled to keep adequate protective equipment in stock, and decried their lack of adequate tests. As recently as early November, diagnostic tests at one home Dr. Tapia regularly visits took more than a week to deliver results, hastening the spread of the virus among unwitting residents.
 Some nursing home residents in the Denver area are getting bounced out of full hospitals because their symptoms aren’t severe, only to rapidly deteriorate and die in their care facilities. “It just happens so fast,” Dr. Tapia said. “There’s no time to send them back.”
 The evening of Nov. 17, Dr. Tapia fielded phone call after phone call from nursing homes brimming with the sick and the scared. Four patients died between 5 p.m. and 8 a.m. “It was the most death pronouncements I’ve ever had to do in one night,” she said.
 Before the pandemic, nursing home residents were already considered a medically neglected population. But the coronavirus has only exacerbated a worrisome chasm of care for older patients. Dr. Tapia is beleaguered by the helplessness she feels at every turn. “Systematically, it makes me feel like I’m failing,” she said. “The last eight months almost broke me.”
 At the end of the summer, Dr. Tapia briefly considered leaving medicine — but she is a single parent to an 11-year-old son, Liam. “I need my M.D. to support my kid,” she said.
  Dr. Gilman, the emergency medicine physician in Yuma, braced himself at the beginning of the pandemic, relying on his stint as a hospital corpsman in Iraq in 2004.
 “In the military, they train you to do sleep deprivation, hikes, marches,” he said. “You train your body, you fight an enemy. I began running every day, getting my lungs strong in case I got the virus. I put a box by the door to put my clothes in, so I wouldn’t spread it to my family.”
 The current crisis turned out to be an unfamiliar and formidable foe that would follow him from place to place.
 Dr. Gilman’s first coronavirus tour began as a resident at New York-Presbyterian at the height of last spring. He came to dread the phone calls to families unable to be near their ailing relatives, hearing “the same shrill cry, two or three times per shift,” he said. Months of chaos, suffering and pain, he said, left him “just down and depressed and exhausted.”
 “I would come home with tears in my eyes, and just pass out,” he said.
  Dr. Gilman canceled his wedding in May. His June graduation commenced on Zoom. He celebrated the end of his residency in his empty apartment next to a pile of boxes.
  Within weeks, he, his fiancée, Maribel, their two daughters and his mother-in-law had relocated to Arizona, where caseloads had just begun to swell. Dr. Gilman hunkered down anew.
 They have weathered the months since in seclusion, keeping the children out of school and declining invitations to mingle, even as their neighbors begin to flock back together and buzz about their holiday plans.
 There are bright spots, he said. The family’s home, which they moved into this summer, is large, and came with a pool. They recently adopted a puppy. Out in the remoteness of small-town Arizona, the desert has delighted them with the occasional roadrunner sighting.
 Since the spring, Dr. Gilman has become a social media tour de force. To document the ongoing crisis, he began publishing journal entries on his website. His  Twitter wall teems with posts commemorating people who lost their lives to Covid-19, and the health workers who have dedicated the past nine months to stemming the tide.
 It’s how he has made sense of the chaos, Dr. Gilman said. What he’s fighting isn’t just the virus itself — but a contagion of disillusionment and misinformation, amid which mask-wearing and distancing continue to flag. “It’s a constant battle, it’s a never-ending war,” he said.
  Nurses and doctors in New York became all too familiar with the rationing of care last spring. No training prepared them for the wrath of the virus, and its aftermath. The month-to-month, day-to-day flailing about as they tried to cope. For some, the weight of the pandemic will have lingering effects.
 Shikha Dass, an emergency room nurse at Mount Sinai Queens, recalled nights in mid-March when her team of eight nurses had to wrangle some 15 patients each — double or triple a typical workload. “We kept getting code after code, and patients were just dying,” Ms. Dass said. The patients quickly outnumbered the available breathing support machines, she said, forcing doctors and nurses to apportion care in a rapid-fire fashion.
 “We didn’t have enough ventilators,” Ms. Dass said. “I remember doing C.P.R. and cracking ribs. These were people from our community — it was so painful.”
  Ms. Dass wrestled with sleeplessness and irritability, sniping at her husband and children. Visions of the dead, strewn across emergency room cots by the dozens, swam through her head at odd hours of the night. Medical TV dramas like Grey’s Anatomy, full of the triggering sounds of codes and beeping machines, became unbearable to watch. She couldn’t erase the memory of the neat row of three refrigerated trailers in her hospital parking lot, each packed with bodies that the morgue was too full to take.
 One morning, after a night shift, Ms. Dass climbed into her red Mini Cooper to start her 20-minute drive home. Her car chugged onto its familiar route; a song from the 2017 film “The Greatest Showman” trickled out. For the first time since the pandemic began, Ms. Dass broke down and began to cry. She called her husband, who was on his way to work; he didn’t pick up. Finally, she reached her best friend.
 “I told her, ‘These people are not going to make it, these people are not going to survive this,’” she said. “We’re there to save a person, save a life, stabilize a person so they can get further management. And here I am, not able to do that.”
 Shortly after, she phoned a longtime friend, Andi Lyn Kornfeld, a psychotherapist who said Ms. Dass was in the throes of “absolute and utter acute PTSD.”
 “I have known Shikha for 13 years,” Ms. Kornfeld said. “She is one of the strongest women I have ever met. And I had never heard her like this.”
  Long gone are the raucous nightly cheers, loud applause and clanging that bounced off buildings and hospital windows in the United States and abroad — the sounds of public appreciation at 7 each night for those on the pandemic’s front line.
 “Nobody’s clapping anymore,” said Dr. Jessica Gold, a psychiatrist at Washington University in St. Louis. “They’re over it.”
 Health workers, once a central part of the coronavirus conversation, have in many ways faded into the background. Some, like Dr. Gilman, in Arizona, have had their salaries slashed as hospitals weigh how to cover costs.
 Many have guiltily recoiled from the “hero” label emblazoned in commercials or ad campaigns, burdened by the death march of the people they could not save and the indiscriminate path of the coronavirus.
 The word “hero” evokes bravery and superhuman strength but leaves little room for empathy, said Dr. Nicole Washington, a psychiatrist in Oklahoma. When portrayed as stalwart saviors, health workers “don’t have the room or right to be vulnerable.”
  Dr. Tapia, the Colorado geriatrician, began taking an antidepressant in September after months of feeling “everything from angry to anxious to furious to just numb and hopeless.” The medication has improved her outlook. But she also worries that these decisions could jeopardize future employment.
 Many state medical boards still ask intrusive questions about physicians’ history of mental health diagnoses or treatments in applications to renew a license —  a disincentive to many doctors who might otherwise seek professional help.
  Being on the front lines doesn’t make health workers stronger or safer than anyone else. “I’m not trying to be a hero. I don’t want to be a hero,” Dr. Gilman said. “I want to be alive.”
 As social bubbles balloon nationwide in advance of the chilly holiday months, health care workers fret on the edges of their communities, worried they are the carriers of contagion.
 Dr. Marshall Fleurant, an internal medicine physician at Emory University, has the sense that his young children, 3 and 4 years old, have grown oddly accustomed to the ritual of his disrobing out of work clothes, from his scrubs to his sneakers, before entering his home.
 “I do not touch or speak to my children before I have taken a shower,” Dr. Fleurant said. “This is just how it is. You do not touch Daddy when he walks in the door.”
 A week of vacation with his family startled him, when he could scoop the little ones up in his arms without fear. “I think they must have thought that was weird,” he said.
  Trapped in a holding pattern as the coronavirus continues to burn across the nation, doctors and nurses have been taking stock of the damage done so far, and trying to sketch out the horizon beyond. On the nation’s current trajectory, they say, the forecast is bleak.
 Jina Saltzman, a physician assistant in Chicago, said she was growing increasingly disillusioned with the nation’s lax approach to penning in the virus.
 While Illinois rapidly reimposed restrictions on restaurants and businesses when cases began to rise, Indiana, where Ms. Saltzman lives, was slower to respond. In mid-November, she was astounded to see crowds of unmasked people in a restaurant as she picked up a pizza. “It’s so disheartening. We’re coming here to work every day to keep the public safe,” she said. “But the public isn’t trying to keep the public safe.”
 Since the spring, Dr. Gilman has watched three co-workers and a cousin die from the virus. Ms. Dass lost a close family friend, who spent three weeks at Mount Sinai Queen’s under her care. When Dr. Fleurant’s aunt died of Covid, “We never got to bury her, never got to pay respects. It was a crushing loss.”
 In state after state, people continue to flood hospital wards, where hallways often provide makeshift beds for the overflow. More than 12 million cases have been recorded since the pandemic took hold in the United States, with the pace of infection accelerating in the last couple months.
 Jill Naiberk, a nurse at the University of Nebraska Medical Center, has spent more of 2020 in full protective gear than out of it. About twice a day, when Ms. Naiberk needs a sip of water, she must completely de-gown, then suit up again.
 Otherwise, “you’re hot and sweaty and stinky,” she said. “It’s not uncommon to come out of rooms with sweat running down your face, and you need to change your mask because it’s wet.”
 It’s her ninth straight month of Covid duty. “My unit is 16 beds. Rarely do we have an open one,” she said. “And when we do have an open bed, it’s usually because somebody has passed away.”
 Many of her I.C.U. patients are young, in their 40s or 50s. “They’re looking at us and saying things like, ‘Don’t let me die’ and ‘I guess I should have worn that mask,’” she said.
 Sometimes she cries on her way home, where she lives alone with her two dogs. Her 79-year-old mother resides just a couple houses away.
  “I keep telling everybody the minute I can safely hug you again, get ready,” she said. “Because I’m never letting go.”
title: 医生和护士在空旷地跑
title_en: Doctors and Nurses Are Running on Empty

自从我处理了一个大型织机以来，已经很长时间了。很长的时间。好像我不记得上一次了吗？这并不是因为我在整个编织方面都非常擅长，而是这样。从70年代中期开始，我一直在编织。如果有错误或搞砸了，我可以向您保证我在职业生涯中某个时候做到了或做到了。成为一名编织者的光荣事情之一就是控制我们做什么和如何处理局势的纯正坚韧。
 当然，到现在为止，你们都已经熟悉了我的女儿和她作为织布工的主要成就。她现在为我工作，负责将我的所有服装样式转换为数字下载。她还负责为我的YouTube频道The Weaver Sews拍摄，制作和编辑我的所有视频，并撰写所有隐藏字幕。 （是的，我发现前几集中有一些拼写错误……）没有她，我无法进入职业生涯的下一个阶段，但这不是我这么做的原因……
 我的女儿Brianna是个真实名字，而不是她在Facebook上使用的名字，去年冬天被问及是否愿意为她在学校附近的马萨诸塞州的旧编织协会做讲座。有关差异解决的讲座定于今年四月举行。当然，我们都知道四月东北发生了什么。世界被取消了。
 由于我的女儿快要28岁了，她在截止日期的压力下尽了最大的努力。 （说实话，我也是如此...）公会上周打电话给她，问她是否愿意远程授课。哈哈哈哈哈！当然，她说是的，那是我那时的年龄。我曾经在工作室的门上贴着标语，说：``是的，然后担心...''
 因此，我的女儿必须建立一个仅作为大纲而存在的整个演讲，并在不到两周的时间里编织所有样本（演讲是下周），还需要为我编辑和创建新内容，以及晚上在她身上进行工作上学（是的，她还在上学以获取兽医技术许可）。但这不是我这么做的原因...。
 Brianna决定编织的一个样本是，探索当您使用差速沉降法时，如果先使用真滑人造丝，经纱和纬纱，然后再使用人造丝湿滑和像设得兰群岛羊毛那样的拖曳纬纱，会发生什么情况。人造丝弯曲的样品虽然具有挑战性，但却是完全成功的。然后，她用设得兰群岛的羊毛缠绕经纱，其想法是将她束缚在人造丝经纱上，并用人造丝纬纱和羊毛纬纱重复该实验，再生产两个样品。
 有一次，她对我说，当我在工作室的另一台织机上编织时，“这太荒谬了，绑在新的经纱上，我可以在一半的时间内开始新鲜，割伤和重新穿线……”和我不能不同意她。我从未发现捆绑新的经线可以节省时间。
我去做别的事情，然后回来，她只在经纱的2/3处扎了一下，然后她搬到另一台织机上，进行其他不同编织结构的样品。她告诉我说她受够了，没有时间浪费在桌子织机上的600个末端上。但这不是我这么做的原因...
 我参加了自己的其他项目，例如为周五的The Weaver Sews Youtube编写脚本。我回来了，决定结束其余的经纱的绑扎，这将是200头。没什么大不了的，我可以在睡眠中做类似的事情。当她指示我打一个方结时，我感到很惊讶，我总是以新手打结，将新结扎起来，但是很久以前我才知道我没有和已故的丈夫吵架，也没有和他的女儿吵架。 。即使我有将近半个世纪的经验……
 我完成了任务，然后将将1 1/2码的粘的设得兰群岛羊毛经线束到经线束上的工作交给了我的女儿。我想我去睡觉了...
 我第二天回来，发现经纱被遗弃了。那是一场彻底的灾难。我认为即使在最糟糕的时候，我也不会遇到这样的麻烦。我之所以承担部分责任，是因为我的女儿自从她第一次学会扔穿梭车以来就一直与我同在。她从来没有机会像大多数织布工（包括我在内）那样平躺在脸上。当她选择听我讲话时，我一直在那里指导她。但这不是我这么做的原因...
 发生的大部分事情是，当她在经纱的后部放张紧杆，并试图将新的粘性设得兰群岛经纱传送到旧的光滑人造丝经纱中时，方结没有固定住，它们就滑了下来。由于某种原因，设得兰群岛的羊毛结头从打结处滑落，最终出现在打浆机的前端，大概是600个结头中的200个。我认为这是我见过的最混乱的经纱获得的奖项。这就是我这么做的部分原因...
  我为我的女儿感到非常抱歉，她正在努力让这次讲座取得成果...但这不是我这么做的原因...
 我感到部分负责，因为我知道当您扎紧经纱时，总是会使用过头打结。我知道很多东西，但我不知道为什么我知道它们。而且因为我的女儿要求打结，所以我不得不。但这不是我这么做的原因...
整个星期四晚上，我躺在我下面车库中的一台织布机上，被乱糟糟的地方困住了。我一直在想，如果那是我的话，我不修好床就不会上床睡觉。就是我，尽管我知道我早上有一个视频要拍摄。我整晚没睡。我的女儿刚搬到另一台织机上，开始进行她计划的另一组样品。
 实际上，我的女儿对发生的事情感到非常沮丧，以至于她什至无法看一下织机。她甚至无法走到工作室的那个区域。她不习惯使用大型织机拧紧螺丝...我在很大程度上保护了她免受此伤害...但这不是我这么做的原因...
 我们在星期五早上停止了一切来拍摄新视频，我有一些电脑/业务资料要参加，但是星期五下午，我坐在织机上，以为，自从我把织机保释出来以来已经很长时间了。发脾气，你知道吗？我真的很想潜入那里并修复它。这就是为什么我想要这样做。
 几年前，我给我的新狗嚼了一根绞纱，将其缠绕成用于我正在从事的项目的纬纱的大头针。我被门铃分散了注意力，当我回到家时，发现绞线在我的织布机上张开，一直下到楼梯上，绞线无助地嚼着。我不敢相信愿意让我把绞线送给他们的织布工的数目，并答应解开最后一码。修复巨大的灾难确实对编织者有吸引力。
  我认为这与在混乱中保持冷静有关。世界上几乎没有什么可以控制的。但是在我们的织机上发生的事情是，我们可以控制。而且，如果我们织机上发生的事情变得非常混乱，那么耐心，坚韧和时间将使其发挥作用。这就是我这样做的原因。
 因此，我在拍摄后的星期五下午开始拍摄，然后我开始重新评估600头以及如何最好地解决问题。裁掉整个东西并重新开始是一种选择，但这意味着浪费完美的1 1/2码设得兰群岛翘曲，我为此付出了很多钱……
 我认为最好的出路是小心地拉出最终在芦苇前面的经纱，因为它们只有1 1/2码，然后在需要的地方重新铺设（因为这是一个不同的沉降经纱，所以有凹痕那里有多达五个末端），然后小心地将它们一一绑在光滑的人造丝经线中。我大概花了10个小时。这确实非常具有挑战性。
我之所以这样做，是因为在使秩序混乱方面，人们感到非常满意。保存项目有些紧张。我很怀疑这是不是可以编织的，设得兰群岛的5个末端，放在织布机上的12个凹痕的芦苇上，看似不切实际，但这不是我可以判断的。我拿起5倍眼镜，一个OTT lite放大镜和一个轻便的钩子，开始穿行.10个小时后，我胜利了。
  正如我所怀疑的那样，经纱在那个假牙上是不可织造的，布赖恩娜不得不拉扯经纱的一些最密实的部分，但是经过反复的咬牙接骨，她设法得到了所需的样品，但这不是我这样做的原因……
  对于所有曾经不得不从地狱中解决扭曲问题的人，请记住，在最终控制那些无法控制的东西方面，有些东西可以治愈，而从完全的混乱中制造出一些东西，则是一种胜利。这就是我这样做的原因。自从我不得不挽救一台大型织机以来已经很长时间了，我喜欢其中的每一分钟。这不是我的事，但我感觉像是执行任务的战士，最终我成功了。任务完成。在最后一刻半的时间里，召集选举总决赛真是令人发指。无论您支持谁，等待都结束了。那里有种解脱，现在作为一个国家，我们可以朝着我希望的共同目标前进。我从地狱中挣扎了十个小时的决心终于结束了。我赢了。
 Brianna确实成功地对新样品进行了编织。正如我所怀疑的，她做到了，必须剔除芦苇最密部分的一些经线。但是她是自己学到的。她还了解到，在绑扎新经线时，应该使用过头打结。但是孩子们会平躺下来然后振作起来，重新评估自己的经历，从而学习。我从来没有其他人要告诉我，所以我通过反复试验学会了艰难的方法，但是编织者的坚韧使我前进。
  要说我为她因进行差分沉降探索而产生的大量样品感到骄傲，这是一种轻描淡写的说法。她从我本来希望避免的丰富经验中学到的东西会使我们俩都失去了最终使我们俩受益的经验。
 因此，下一次您将面对来自地狱的扭曲，喝一杯，然后先潜水。有一些个人的胜利，只有织工才能理解…
note_en:
It has been a very long time since I’ve dealt with a major loom screw up.  A really long time.  Like I don’t remember the last time?  And it isn’t because I’m so very good at this whole weaving thing, but it sort of is.  I’ve been weaving since the mid 70’s.  If there is a mistake, or screw up, I can assure you I made it or did it at some point in my career.  One of the glorious things about being a weaver is the pure tenacity that controls what we do and how we approach a situation.
 Of course by now, you are all familiar with my daughter and her major accomplishments as a weaver.  She works for me now, and is responsible for converting all of my garment patterns into  digital downloads.  She is also responsible for filming, producing and editing all of my videos for my YouTube channel,   The Weaver Sews and writing all of the Closed Captioning.  (Yes, I caught that there were a couple of misspellings in previous episodes…)  I couldn’t have moved into this next portion of my professional life without her, but that’s not why I did it…
 My daughter, Brianna, yes she has a real name, not the one she uses on Facebook, was asked back last winter if she would give a lecture to her old weaving guild in MA, near where she went to school.  The lecture, on differential sett, was scheduled for April of this year.  And of course we all know what happened in the northeast by April.  The world was cancelled.
 Since my daughter is a soon to be 28 year old millennial, she does her best work under deadline pressure.  (Truth be told, so do I…)  The guild called her last week and asked if she would be willing to give the lecture remotely.  Hahahahah!  Of course she said yes, which is what I would have done at that age.  I use to have a sign on  my studio door that said, “Say yes, then worry…”
 So my daughter had to build an entire lecture that only existed as an outline, and weave all the samples in less than two weeks (the lecture is next week), plus edit and create new content for me, plus work in the evenings on her schooling, (yes, she is still in school to get her vet tech license).  But that’s not why I did it….
 One of the samples Brianna decided to weave, was exploring what happens when you use differential sett with really slippery rayon, warp and weft, and then slippery rayon warp and a dragging kind of weft like Shetland wool.  The sample with the slippery rayon warp, though challenging, was completely successful.  She then wound a warp with the Shetland wool, and the idea was she would tie into the rayon warp and repeat the experiment with a rayon weft and a wool weft, producing an additional two samples.
 At one point, she said to me, as I was weaving on another of the looms in the studio, “This is ridiculous, tying in a new warp, I could have started fresh, sleying and rethreading in half the time…”  and I couldn’t disagree with her.  I’ve never found tying in a new warp to be a time saver.
 I went off to do something else and came back and she had only tied in about 2/3rds of the warp, and she moved onto a different loom to do other samples of different weave structures.  She told me that she was fed up and didn’t have the time to waste tying in 600 ends on a table loom.  But that’s not why I did it…
 I went off to other projects of my own, like writing the script for Friday’s   The Weaver Sews Youtube installment.  I came back and decided to finish tying in the rest of the warp, which would have been 200 ends.  It wasn’t a big deal, and I can do stuff like that in my sleep.  I was surprised when she directed me to make a square knot, I had always tied in new warps with an overhand knot, but I learned long ago that I didn’t argue with my late husband, and I don’t argue with his daughter.  Even though I have almost a half century of experience…
 I finished the task and then turned the job of beaming the 1  1/2 yard warp of sticky Shetland wool, onto the warp beam, over to my daughter.  I think I went off to bed…
 I came back the next day to find the warp abandoned.  It was a complete disaster.  I don’t think even at my worst I’ve ever had a mess like that.  Partly I take some responsibility because my daughter has worked along side of me since she first learned to throw a shuttle.  She never had the opportunity to fall flat on her face, like most weavers, including me, have had to do.  I’ve always been there to guide her, when she chooses to listen to me.  But that’s not why I did it…
 Largely what happened, is that when she put tensioning bars in the back of the warp, and tried to beam the new sticky Shetland warp into the old slick rayon warp, the square knots didn’t hold, they slipped right out.  And for some reason, the Shetland wool ends, that slipped out of the knots, ended up in the front of the beater, probably about 200 of the 600 ends.  I think this wins an award for the most messed up warp I’ve ever seen.  That’s partly why I did it…
  I felt really sorry for my daughter, she was trying so hard to see this lecture into fruition…  But that’s not why I did it…
 I felt partly responsible because I knew that when you tie in a warp, you always use overhand knots.  There are a lot of things I know, but I don’t know why I know them.  And because my daughter requested square knots, I obliged.  But that’s not why I did it…
 I laid awake all Thursday night haunted by the mess on one of the looms in the garage right underneath of me.  I kept thinking, if that were me, I wouldn’t have gone to bed without fixing it.  But that’s me, even though I knew I had a video to shoot in the morning. I didn’t sleep the whole night.  My daughter just moved to a different loom, and started on a different group of samples she had been planning.
 In fact my daughter was so upset by what happened that she couldn’t even look at the loom. She couldn’t even walk over to that area of the studio.   She is not use to having major loom screw ups…  I’ve largely protected her from that…  But that’s not why I did it…
 We stopped everything to shoot the new video Friday morning, and I had some computer/business stuff to attend to, but Friday afternoon, I sat at the loom and thought, it has been a very long time since I’ve bailed a loom out of a major temper tantrum, and you know what?  I really wanted to just dive in there and fix it.  That’s why I wanted to do it.
 A couple of years ago, I had my new to me dog chew up a skein of yarn that was being wound into pirns for the weft yarn for a project I was working on.  I got distracted by the doorbell, and when I returned I found  the skein stretched around my loom, and all the way down the stairs, and the skein chewed beyond help.  I can’t believe the number of weaver’s who offered to have me send them the skein and promised to untangle every last yard.  There is something about fixing a monumental disaster that is really appealing for a weaver.
  I sort of think that it has to do with creating calm in chaos.  There is so little in the world that we have any control over.  But what happens at our looms, that thing, we have control over.  And if what happens on our looms becomes total chaos, then patience, tenacity, and time will make it work.  That’s why I did it.
 So I started Friday afternoon, after the shoot, and I began to reassess the 600 ends and how to best resolve the mess.  Cutting the whole thing off and starting over was an option, but it would mean wasting a perfectly good 1 1/2 yard Shetland warp, that I paid good money for…
 I decided that the best way out, was to carefully pull the warps that ended up in front of the reed, since they were only 1 1/2 yards, and resleying them where required (because this was a differential sett warp, there were dents where there were as many as five ends) and then carefully tying them back into the slippery rayon warps that went through the heddles, one by one.  I probably spent 10 hours.  This was really really challenging.
  I did it because there is something intensely satisfying about bringing order to chaos.  There is something intense about saving a project.  I had my doubts that this was even weaveable, 5 ends of Shetland in a 12 dent reed on a table loom didn’t see realistic, but that wasn’t for me to judge.  I grabbed my 5X glasses, a magnifying OTT lite, and a sley hook and started in.  10 hours later I was triumphant.
  As I suspected, the warp was unweaveable at that dentage, Brianna had to pull some of the densest parts of the warp, but after much bitching and kvetching, she managed to get the sample she needed, but that’s not why I did it…
  For all of you out there who have ever had to deal with the warp from hell, remember that there is something healing in finally controlling that which would not be controlled, something triumphant about making something from total chaos.  And that’s  why I did it.  It has been a long time since I’ve had to bail out a major loom screw up, and I loved every minute of it.  It wasn’t my screw up, but I felt like a warrior on a mission and I was ultimately successful.  Mission accomplished.  It was sort of poignant that in the middle of the last inch and a half, that the election finals were called.  No matter who you supported, the wait is over.  And there is a sort of relief there, and now we as a nation can move forward to what I hope is a common goal.  My ten hours of determination over a warp from hell was finally over.  And I won.
 Brianna did manage to beam and weave the new samples.  She did as I suspected have to cull some of the warps in the densest part of the reed.  But she learned that on her own.  And she also learned that when tying in a new warp, you should use overhand knots.  But kids learn by falling flat on their faces and picking themselves up and reevaluating the experience.  I never had anyone to tell me otherwise, so I learned the hard way, by trial and error, but that weaver’s tenacity kept me moving forward.
  To say that I’m so proud of the body of samples she has produced as a result of this exploration in differential sett would be an understatement.  And what she has learned from things that I would have loved to have prevented from my vast experience would have deprived both of us from an experience that ultimately benefitted both of us.
 So next time you are faced with the warp from hell, have a drink, and dive in head first.  There is some personal triumph that no one else but a weaver will understand…
title: 那不是我这么做的原因
title_en: That’s not why I did it

在《华尔街日报》（Wall Street Journal）报道说，Salesforce进行了收购该公司的谈判之后，Slack的股价周三上涨。
 据《华尔街日报》报道，Salesforce举行了收购公司的谈判，Slack的股票周三上涨了32％。
   根据这份报告，这笔交易可能会使Slack的市值超过其170亿美元的市值。然而，根据《华尔街日报》的报道，Slack的市值激增至超过200亿美元。
 Salesforce一直在疯狂收购，利用近年来的市值增长来收购成长中的公司。它在2018年以65亿美元的价格收购了MuleSoft，以帮助连接云应用程序，这是该公司当时最大的一笔交易。次年，它在Tableau上花费了两倍多的资金，以153亿美元收购了数据可视化公司。
 Slack将成为该行业有史以来最大的软件交易之一。 IBM将在2019年以340亿美元收购Red Hat，2016年以270亿美元收购LinkedIn以及2014年以190亿美元收购WhatsApp的交易中排名。
 Wedbush Securities的Dan Ives告诉Salesforce首席执行官马克·贝尼奥夫（Marc Benioff）表示：“这将使贝尼奥夫（Benioff＆Co.）改变游戏规则，进一步扩大其协作引擎和产品覆盖范围，因为云计算在企业中的支出将不断增加。”
 据《华尔街日报》报道，不能保证达成交易，谈判可能会失败。
通过其Teams产品与Slack竞争的Microsoft股价也略微下跌。
 微软已经与Salesforce在跟踪客户的软件方面展开竞争。据报道，微软曾考虑购买Salesforce，但没有达成任何协议。后来，Microsoft启动了Teams通信应用程序。
 Slack在2019年寻求成为上市公司时说：“我们的主要竞争对手是Microsoft Corporation。” Microsoft具有庞大的客户群，已经能够转换为Teams，Salesforce可以为Slack提供类似的好处。
 Slack上一次在2019年10月表示，它每天有超过1200万活跃用户。
note_en:
Shares of Slack jumped Wednesday following a Wall Street Journal report that Salesforce held talks to buy the company.
 Shares of  Slack rose as much as 32% Wednesday following a Wall Street Journal report that  Salesforce held talks to buy the company.
   The deal would likely value Slack at more than its market cap of $17 billion, according to the report. However, Slack's market cap ballooned to more than $20 billion following the WSJ report.
 Salesforce has been on an  acquisition spree, taking advantage of its market cap growth in recent years to buy growing companies. It acquired MuleSoft for $6.5 billion in 2018, the company's biggest deal ever at the time, to help connect cloud applications. The following year it spent more than twice that amount on Tableau, acquiring the data visualization company for $15.3 billion.
 Slack would be one of the biggest software deals ever for the industry. It would rank among  IBM's $34 billion purchase of Red Hat in 2019, Microsoft's $27 billion purchase of LinkedIn in 2016 and  Facebook's $19 billion purchase of WhatsApp in 2014.
 "This would be a game changer move for Benioff & Co. to further build out its collaboration engine and product footprint as cloud spending ramps across the enterprise," Wedbush Securities' Dan Ives told CNBC, referring to Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff.
 A deal is not guaranteed and the talks could fall through, the Wall Street Journal reported.
  Shares of  Microsoft, which competes with Slack through its  Teams offering, also dipped slightly on the report.
 Microsoft already competes with Salesforce in software for tracking customers. Microsoft reportedly looked at buying Salesforce but no deal came about. Later, Microsoft launched the Teams communication app.
 "Our primary competitor is currently Microsoft Corporation," Slack said when it sought to become a public company in 2019. Microsoft has a large customer base it has been able to convert to Teams, and Salesforce could give Slack a similar benefit.
 Slack last said in October 2019 that it had more than  12 million daily active users.

我有技术背景（我是经过培训的物理学家），并且在我刚开始从事销售工作时有0年的经验。在过去的几年中，我与许多创始人讨论了销售和增长，并注意到我们都在为同样的问题而苦苦挣扎。我以为我将分享我个人学习的10项最重要的事情，以及其他创始人多年来与我分享的内容。
   这听起来很陈词滥调，这对销售而言是最重要的。您与潜在客户和客户互动的所有过程中都会充满热情。如果您充满激情并真正相信自己所建造和销售的产品，那么其他一切都会变得容易得多。大量的销售是关于传递热情的！
   解决此问题的最简单方法是解决您自己的问题。如果您要解决自己的问题，那么您就是所构建解决方案的客户。如果不是这种情况，则进行一些市场前景调查，并从您认为将从正在构建的解决方案中受益最大的公司和人员那里获得初步反馈。收集反馈并建立理想的客户档案。
   过度销售将导致很多头痛和保留问题。不要承诺您不打算建立和调整潜在客户期望的功能。
   询问您正在考虑购买的竞争对手产品和其他工具的演示。您可以从他们的优点中学习（在通话开始时设定议程，建立融洽的关系，跟进...），而不是那么好（杂乱无章，开会迟到，过于激进...），以及将这些经验应用于您自己的销售过程。
   记录您的演示并听他们讲。您会在说话方式中发现很多烦人的方面，并且随着时间的推移会不断完善。在您的第一个电话中，您会从客户那里获得很多反馈，使用这些反馈来调整您的消息传递并提高音调。
   销售可能会困难重重，如果您以前从未做过，那就尤其困难。您将面临拒绝并每天失望。放开自我，不要个人反馈。坚持不懈！
您将无法完成所有交易。以结构化的方式记录丢失的交易，以便您分析由于时间，价格，竞争对手等原因而从渠道漏掉的百分比。通过共享新功能来培育已完成的丢失交易，并每季度重新审视它们。
   您的前景很聪明，其中大多数人都和您一样了解市场。您无需浪费说话或贬低竞争对手。专注于自己的信息以及与众不同的地方。
   不良客户会占用您大量时间，并可能导致您的产品/公司走错方向。作为创始人，让客户离开并不是一个容易的决定，但是有时候最好分开。
   庆祝每位新客户！很容易在销售和建立公司的日常工作中根深蒂固，而忘了庆祝胜利。与您的联合创始人和团队一起庆祝胜利。
  作为创始人，您面临的挑战是销售不受任何品牌知名度支持的产品，并且该产品很可能完成了一半。即使您从未出售过产品，但如果您相信自己的产品和使命，您仍然可以比销售人员高出10倍。
      我是经过培训的物理学家。 Y Combinator校友。如果您想与初创企业，销售，增长或科学领域进行交流，请随时通过mica@amplemarket.com向我发送电子邮件。 🙂
note_en:
I have a technical background (I am a Physicist by training) and had 0 experience when I first started doing sales. Over the past years I have talked with many founders about sales and growth and noticed that we were all struggling with the same problems. I thought I would share the 10 most important things I personally learned and other founders have shared with me over the years.
   As cliché as this might sound it's the most important thing when it comes to sales. Your enthusiasm comes across in all of your interactions with prospects and customers. If you are passionate and truly believe in what you are building and selling then everything else becomes much easier. A lot of sales is about transferring enthusiasm!
   The easiest way to go about this is to solve your own problem. If you are solving your own problem then you are a customer of the solution you are building. If this is not the case then do some market prospecting and get initial feedback from the companies and people whom you believe would benefit the most from the solution you are building. Gather this feedback and build your ideal customer profile.
   Overselling will result in a lot of headaches and retention problems. Don't promise features you are not planning to build and align your prospects expectations with.
   Ask for demos of competitor products and other tools you are thinking about buying. You can learn from what they do well (setting an agenda at the beginning of a call, building rapport, following up, ...) and not so well (rambling, being late for meetings, being too aggressive, ...) and apply those learnings to your own sales process.
   Record your demos and listen to them. You will find a lot of annoying aspects in the way you talk and you will refine these over time. You will get a lot of feedback from customers during your first calls, use that feedback to make adjustments to your messaging and improve your pitch.
   Sales can be a grind and it's especially tough if you have never done it before. You will be faced with rejection and get let down every single day. Let go of your ego and don't take feedback personally. Persevere!
   You will not be able to close all of your deals. Take notes for the lost deals in a structured way so you can analyse the percentage that fell out of the funnel due to timing, pricing, competitors, etc... Nurture the closed lost deals by sharing new features and revisit them every quarter.
   Your prospects are smart and most of them know the market as well as you do. You don't need to trash talk or belittle your competitors. Focus on your own message and what makes you different.
   Bad customers will take a lot of your time and might steer your product/company in the wrong direction. As a founder letting a customer go is not an easy decision but sometimes it's just best to part ways.
   Celebrate every new customer! It's easy to get too ingrained in the daily grind of sales and building a company and forget to celebrate the wins. Celebrate every win with your co-founders and your team.
  As a founder you are faced with the challenges of selling a product that is not supported by any brand awareness and is most likely half complete. Even if you have never sold before, you can still be 10x better than they are as a salesperson if you believe in your product and your mission.
      I am Physicist by training. Y Combinator Alumnus. If you want to talk startups, sales, growth or science feel free to email me at mica@amplemarket.com. 🙂

note_en:
WhatsApp has just submitted a new update through the  Google Play Beta Program, bringing the version up to 2.20.206.3. What’s new in this update? Discover all details below!
   I’ve the same version but I don’t see this news, why?
 It’s not possible. WhatsApp has introduced this feature for beta testers having the 2.20.206.3 update.
  WhatsApp is introducing a new feature for beta testers, already available once updating to the latest 2.20.206.3 update. Actually, when you report a contact to WhatsApp, WhatsApp elaborates your report considering several factors, like the number of reports received for that phone number from different users, if the account of the reported user is new and it has already received some reports, etc… With this update, WhatsApp is introducing a new way to verify if a report is reliable: receiving a copy of recent messages from that chat. WhatsApp  never receives your messages without your permissions. If you decide to report a contact, you agree to forward a copy to WhatsApp of the recent messages from that chat.
     This feature is available TODAY for Android beta testers and it will be following soon for iOS beta testers in the next few weeks.

迭戈·马拉多纳被认为是有史以来最伟大的足球运动员之一，享年60岁。
 他的律师说，这个月进行脑外科手术的阿根廷人死于心脏病。该国宣布全国哀悼三天。
  马拉多纳（Maradona）在1986年的世界杯上为阿根廷带来了荣耀，当时担任队长的他表现出了无可比拟的技能，创造力，力量和渴望。在2-1英格兰的四分之一决赛中，他也取得了有史以来最大的进球，这场比赛中前锋还用臭名昭著的“上帝之手”表现出了他更黑暗，调皮的一面。
  马拉多纳在俱乐部一级也取得了成功，最著名的是那不勒斯，他在1987年带领了他们的第一个意甲冠军。第二次是1990年，接着是1987年的意大利杯和1989年的欧洲联盟杯，这就是球员的影响力在一个曾经住在意大利北部强队，尤其是尤文图斯，米兰和国际米兰的阴影下的俱乐部，那不勒斯在2000年宣布他们将退役他的10号球衣。
 然而，正是在他在那不勒斯（Naples）的7年中，马拉多纳（Maradona）对可卡因的依赖开始受到控制。 1991年-离开那不勒斯那年，他因涉嫌违禁药物而被停赛15个月，三年后，他的麻黄碱呈阳性反应，因此被淘汰出美国世界杯。
 从那以后，马拉多纳的个人生活逐渐失去控制，在2000年和2004年，他因心脏病入院，这是第二次要求使用呼吸器来正常呼吸。次年，他接受了胃旁路手术以帮助阻止肥胖。
 然而，在巅峰时期几乎是超自然的球场上，这些都无法掩盖他的才华。
迭戈·阿曼多·马拉多纳（Diego Armando Maradona）于1960年生于布宜诺斯艾利斯，是一名神童，他加入了阿根廷青年青年队Los Cebollitas，10岁那年，他在他们取得令人难以置信的136连胜的过程中发挥了关键作用，这反过来导致他在16岁生日之前首次登台登台。
 此后不久-1977年2月27日-马拉多纳首次亮相阿根廷，以65分钟的替补身份在博卡青年队的故乡拉·邦博纳拉对阵匈牙利的友谊赛中出场，马拉多纳于1981年加入了马拉多纳。俱乐部，但那时候他击败了竞争对手河床队（River Plate）取得了令人惊叹的独奏进球，并帮助博卡赢得了冠军。
  鉴于他的快速发展和始终如一的指挥能力，马拉多纳很快就被欧洲最大的俱乐部所吸引，并在1982年因参加了他在西班牙举行的第一届世界杯足球赛而以500万英镑的创世界纪录转会费加盟巴塞罗那。
 然而，他努力地向加泰罗尼亚人展示自己的最佳状态，部分原因是他在1983年9月因“毕尔巴鄂屠夫” Andoni Goicoechea的钓具而受伤，脚踝受伤。但马拉多纳最终康复，并于1984年加入那不勒斯。两年后，墨西哥举行了世界杯，从那一刻起，他就确立了自己的实力。
 阿根廷队长每场比赛每一分钟都打进一球，打进5球，为另外5球提供助攻。在整个比赛中，他都是至高无上，最有活力和最激动人心的球员。在阿兹台克体育场对阵英格兰的第二个进球中，他喘不过气来，当时他在中途扭过两名球员，然后冲过另一对，并在压力下来自特里·布彻（Terry Butcher）的弓步，绕过彼得·席尔顿（Peter Shilton），然后将球传给了空网。这是与勇气相关的惊人技能展示，毫无疑问，罢工仍被某些人称为“本世纪的目标”。
  四分钟前，马拉多纳打进了自己的第一场比赛。阿根廷10号手显然向希尔顿（Shilton）扑了过去，两人挑战高球，但尽管鲍比·罗布森（Bobby Robson）的男子提出抗议，但进球仍然存在。 “我永远不会原谅他，”布彻（Butcher）在2008年说。在这种情况下很难原谅和忘记。”
 马拉多纳还参加了另外两次世界杯比赛：在意大利队90时，他带领队获得亚军；在美国94时，他在与尼日利亚队比赛后被送回家，事实证明这是他最后一次出场。马拉多纳总共封顶91次，打入34球，这是1994年世界杯阿根廷对阵希腊的最后一场比赛。
在俱乐部级别，马拉多纳（Maradona）在离开那不勒斯（Napoli）后加入塞维利亚（Sevilla），然后返回家乡效力于纽维尔（Newell）的老男孩（Old Boys）和博卡（Boca），他于1997年退休。
  在个人争斗之后，他管理了阿根廷两年，将他们带到了2010年南非世界杯，在那里，一支由胡安·塞巴斯蒂安·维隆，卡洛斯·特维斯和莱昂内尔·梅西组成的团队进入了八强。一年后，他离开了国家队的职位，负责了迪拜俱乐部Al-Wasl的工作，随后继续担任多个职位，最近是阿根廷第一分部球队Gimnasia y Esgrima的经理。
 但是马拉多纳将以其出色的表现而被人们铭记。在互联网民意测验以及国际足联官员，教练和球员的提名相结合后，他五次被评为年度南美足球先生，并与贝利一起在2000年被评为国际足联年度最佳球员。马拉多纳赢得了全民投票。
note_en:
Diego Maradona, regarded as one of the greatest footballers of all time, has died aged 60.
 The Argentinian, who  had brain surgery this month, died of a heart attack, his lawyer said. The country declared three days of national mourning.
  Maradona inspired Argentina to World Cup glory in 1986 when as captain he displayed a level of skill, creativity, strength and desire arguably not seen before or since. In the 2-1 quarter-final victory over England he also  scored perhaps the greatest goal of all time, a match in which the forward also showed his darker, mischievous side with the infamous “ Hand of God”.
  Maradona also achieved success at club level, most notably with Napoli, whom he led to their first Serie A title in 1987. A second followed in 1990, alongside an Italian Cup in 1987 and a Uefa Cup in 1989, and such was the player’s impact at a club that previously had lived in the shadow of Italy’s northern powerhouses, particularly Juventus, Milan and Internazionale, that Napoli announced in 2000 they were retiring his No 10 shirt.
 It was during his seven years in Naples, however, that Maradona’s addiction to cocaine took grip. He was hit with a 15-month suspension for drug violations in 1991 – the year he left Napoli – and, three years later, was thrown out of the World Cup in the USA after testing positive for ephedrine.
 From there Maradona’s personal life spiralled out control and in 2000 and 2004 he was hospitalised for heart problems, the second time requiring the use of a respirator to breathe properly. The following year he underwent gastric-bypass surgery to help stem his obesity.
 None of that, however, could overshadow his talents on the pitch which, at their peak, were almost supernatural.
  Born in Buenos Aires on 30 October 1960, Diego Armando Maradona was a child prodigy and having joined Los Cebollitas, a youth team of Argentinos Juniors, at the age of 10 he played a key role in them going on an incredible 136-game winning streak, which in turn led to him making his debut for the senior side just before his 16th birthday.
 Shortly afterwards – on 27 February 1977 – Maradona made his debut for  Argentina, coming on as a 65th-minute substitute in a friendly against Hungary at La Bombonera, the home of Boca Juniors, whom Maradona joined in 1981. He spent only one season at the club but in that time scored a stunning solo goal against archrivals River Plate and helped Boca to win the title.
  Given his rapid development and consistently commanding displays, it was no surprise Maradona was soon being courted by Europe’s biggest clubs and in 1982, having featured at his first World Cup, in Spain, he joined Barcelona for a then world record fee of £5m.
 He struggled to show his best form for the Catalans, however, partly because of the broken ankle he suffered in September 1983 from a  tackle by the “Butcher of Bilbao”, Andoni Goicoechea. But Maradona eventually recovered and in 1984 joined Napoli. Two years later came the World Cup in Mexico and the moment he established himself as a genuine great.
 Argentina’s captain played every minute of every game, scoring five goals and providing assists for five others. He was supreme throughout, the most dynamic and exciting player at the tournament, and took the breath away with his second goal against England at the Azteca Stadium, when he twisted past two players on the halfway line before bursting past another pair and, under pressure from Terry Butcher’s lunge, went around Peter Shilton before passing the ball into an empty net. It was a stunning display of skill allied to courage and it little wonder the strike is still refereed to by some as “the goal of the century.”
  Four minutes earlier Maradona scored his first of the game. Argentina’s No 10 clearly punched the ball past Shilton as the pair challenged for a high ball but, despite protests from Bobby Robson’s men, the goal stood. “ I’ll never forgive him,” Butcher said in 2008. “It’s not nice when you lose a World Cup quarter-final under those circumstances. It’s very hard to forgive and forget in the circumstances.”
 Maradona took part in two further World Cups – at Italia 90, when the team he captained finished as runners-up, and at USA 94, when he was sent home after playing against Nigeria in what proved to be his last appearance for his country. In total Maradona was capped 91 times, scoring 34 goals, the last against Greece in Argentina’s opening game at the 1994 World Cup.
 At club level Maradona joined Sevilla following his departure from Napoli, before returning to his homeland to play for Newell’s Old Boys and Boca, where he retired in 1997.
  Following his personal battles he  managed Argentina for two years, taking them to the 2010 World Cup in South Africa, where a team containing Juan Sebastian Verón, Carlos Tevez and Lionel Messi  reached the quarter-finals. A year later, and having left the national-team post, he took charge of the Dubai-based club Al-Wasl before going on to hold a number of positions, most recently manager of the Argentinian first division side Gimnasia y Esgrima.
 But it is for his playing exploits that Maradona will be best remembered. Having been named South American Footballer of the Year on five occasions, he was in 2000 named Fifa’s Player of the Century alongside Pelé following a combination of an internet poll and nominations from Fifa officials, coaches and players. Maradona won the popular vote.
title: 迭戈·马拉多纳（Diego Maradona）是有史以来最伟大的足球运动员之一，享年60岁
title_en: Diego Maradona, one of the greatest footballers of all time, dies aged 60

The holiday seasons is upon us and with that comes stress, anxiety, and the airing of grievances. And this year is worse. Instead of watching a yule log smolder in a warm hearth, we’re all stuck in our homes watching the world burn.
 Try this interactive dumpster fire instead of yelling into the void. The instructions are simple. Just email a note or picture and a contraption incinerates your asseveration. It’s cathartic, but takes a minute. About 20 minutes after emailing my issues, I’m still waiting to see it burn.
 Do it as a family or with your cats. Roast Covid-19. Burn politics. Send a picture of your QAnon-loving uncle. The fire doesn’t care. Everything burns eventually.
