note:
巴西农业仅用了几十年的时间便将其热带稀树草原腹地-塞拉多（Cerrado）转变为农业强国。
 农民和不断发展的农业综合企业庆祝大豆和牛肉的销售量增长，以及与之一起成长的道路和城镇。但是环保主义者和塞拉多社区表示，取得进展的代价是森林砍伐，土地抢占，暴力和传统土地的流失。
 MapBiomas监控项目计算得出，塞拉多的近一半植被（包括灌木丛，草地和森林）已用于农业（44％）。在巴西极右翼总统贾尔·博尔索纳罗（Jair Bolsonaro）的大力支持下，其强大的农业游说团正在其200万平方公里的其余地区迅速消失。仅去年一年，就清理了6,500平方公里-自2001年以来砍伐的283,000平方公里的森林，草地和灌木丛增加了。
  到目前为止，今年以来，塞拉多已经收到了超过61,000次火灾警报，而在博尔索纳罗（Bolsonaro）上台之前，整个2018年全年只有39,000起警报，这引发了人们对其未来的担忧。它是一个巨大的重要碳汇，在其土壤和深层根系中存储着数百吨公顷的土地。
 巴西的塞拉多生物群落是一种热带热带大草原，对角线延伸到巴西中部，占地200万平方公里，约占该国22％的土地，以及玻利维亚和巴拉圭的部分地区。
 根据巴西环境部的说法，生物多样性丰富的塞拉多拥有11,620种植物，1,200种鱼类和837种鱼类，其200种哺乳动物包括美洲虎，食蚁兽，丽亚鸟类和tap。但是，由于其一半以上的景观（草地灌木丛和干旱森林）已经转化为农业，因为它为中国，欧洲和其他市场生产大豆。据路透社报道，该地区在2008年至2018年间失去了105,000平方公里的原生植被，比拥有更多法律保护的亚马逊多了50％。
 根据《科学》杂志发布的一项国际研究，塞拉多的大约27,000处房产“极有可能是非法采伐”，其中48％的房产不符合巴西森林法规的要求，例如保护20％的森林。他们的土地（亚马逊地区为80％）。研究发现，巴西亚马逊和塞拉多生物群落向欧盟出口的大豆约占20％，牛肉出口约占17％，“可能被非法森林砍伐所污染”。
美国达特茅斯学院的研究人员在《自然可持续发展》杂志上发表的另一项研究发现，土地清理改变了塞拉多的天气。玉米生长季节温度升高，蒸散量下降。
 去年，在《生态与保护观点》上发表的一篇文章中，科学家警告说：“气候变化可能导致整个塞拉多生物群落的几种哺乳动物物种局部灭绝”。
 “塞拉多就像巴西的储水箱。这正在影响河流，重要河流的河流状态。”米纳斯吉拉斯州联邦大学环境模型教授Britaldo Soares-Filho说。 “塞拉多（Cerrado）是生物多样性的热点地区，承受着很大的压力。”
 塞拉多（Cerrado）遍布巴西中部，对角线环绕，覆盖该国约22％的土地。其丰富的野生动植物包括11,620种植物，以及200多种哺乳动物，包括美洲虎，食蚁兽，丽亚鸟类和tap。
  1960年，该国的首都移至塞拉多中部的巴西利亚，而中西部的州开始充斥着许多农场，该地区被忽略了多年。 1964年至1985年，巴西的军事专政发起了一些计划，以使塞拉多农业现代化，改善其基础设施并在偏远地区殖民。
 1970年代后期，日本和巴西启动了一项计划，以帮助该地区的殖民和发展，以保证大豆供应。嘉吉（Cargill）和邦吉（Bunge）等大型跨国贸易商进驻。中国的投资帮助铺平了道路，改善了港口。 “他们创造了有利于出口的结构，”坎皮纳格兰德联邦大学社会学教授罗伯托·米兰达（Roberto Miranda）说。
 甚至比塞拉多更大的亚马逊也正在被越来越多地利用。但是那次破坏正引起国际关注和关注：与此同时，塞拉多仍处于雷达之下。在2017年的一篇论文中，Soares-Filho和其他研究人员计算出，虽然亚马逊地区的49％位于保护区（例如保护单位和土著保护区），但塞拉多地区仅占7.7％。根据巴西的2012年《森林法》，农民必须将亚马逊地区80％的土地作为“法律保护区”，但在塞拉多大部分地区只有20％。 Soares-Filho说：“塞拉多关系很差。”结果，农业综合企业喜欢它。
该地区的“农业前沿”是马托比巴广阔的地区，前总统迪尔玛·罗塞夫（Dilma Rousseff）的马托比巴开发机构就坐落在这里。鲁塞夫在2015年的发布会上说：“在农业领域，我们仍然有能力采摘低垂的果实，同时还能扩大树势。这就是为什么这是一个非常特殊的时刻。”
  搬到该地区的农民很现实。保罗·达尔托·内托（Paulo Dalto Neto）于1997年来到这里耕种，他说：“我们的野心是永远致富，但我们希望生活中有所收获。”在这个干旱，荒凉的地区，土地价格便宜，但他已经能够改善自己的生活。土壤，开发当地道路并种植高产耐寒大豆菌株。
  失败者是塞拉多（Cerrado）的传统社区，他们通常没有自己几代人使用的合法土地所有权。大豆农民珍视这块土地，在某些情况下，不道德的土地交易者可以通过伪造文件来购买或偷窃。 “这里的大豆给我们带来了很少的工作……并给我们带来了许多劣势，”现年63岁的Piareí的Melancias社区的Juarez de Souza说。
 迪苏扎（De Souza）的社区现在正在进行一场法律斗争，以证明自己的土地所有权，而根据迪苏扎（De Souza）的说法，当地人用水和捕鱼的河道已被农药污染。他说，他已经受到土地抢夺者的威胁，他们正在寻找土地出售给更大的农民。他说：“对他们而言，大豆就是金子。” “有很多钱。”
  此外，土地利用的变化现在正在改变该地区的气候。他说，降雨少，温度高。他说：研究表明，森林砍伐是干旱和河流行为不稳定的原因。同时，农民们将其天然植被一点一点地砍掉。
  问题在于，尽管巴西因冠状病毒大流行而在经济衰退中挣扎，迄今为止已导致16.9万人死亡。查克，塞拉多的大豆生产（主要用于动物饲料）正在蓬勃发展，并且有望达到2020-2021年收成创纪录的1.35亿吨。巴西农业部长Tereza Cristina Dias在11月10日发布推文说：“这是我们农业综合企业的力量。”巴西预计11月份的牛肉出口将创历史新高–在塞拉多州，该国饲养的牛只占最大份额。
 公共账户分析师法比奥•克莱因（FábioKlein）表示，巴西目前面临着严重的赤字，由于向数千万人提供的紧急大流行支付以及创纪录的14％的失业率使情况更加恶化，其蓬勃发展的大豆和牛肉等农产品的出口缓解了人们的压力。在圣保罗的咨询机构Tendências。 “农业是一个竞争非常激烈的部门，其产生的收入虽然集中，但溢出到整个经济领域，对国家和政府都有好处。”
总部位于戈亚尼亚Cerrado的美国农业综合企业顾问科里·梅尔比（Kory Melby）表示，巴西大豆的高蛋白和高油脂含量使其在巴西最大的大豆客户中国特别受欢迎。他说：“国际和国内市场告诉巴西，尽管巴西正在尽其所能迅速扩张，但我们希望她的扩张速度更快。” “这让我震惊。”
 问题在于，尽管塞拉多看上去像是低落的果实，但从长远来看，巴西正在失去更多的珍贵森林和野生动植物。他们的损失将付出沉重的代价。
 订阅《动物农场》每月更新，以获取全球最佳农业和美食故事的摘要，并与我们的调查保持同步。您可以通过animalsfarmed@theguardian.com将您的故事和想法发送给我们。
note_en:
It took just a few decades for Brazilian agriculture to transform its tropical savannah hinterlands – the Cerrado – into an agricultural powerhouse.
 Farmers and the growing agribusiness sector celebrated rising sales of soya and beef, and the roads and towns that grew up with them. But environmentalists and Cerrado communities say  the advances came at the price of roaring deforestation, land grabbing, violence and the loss of traditional lands.
 Nearly half (44%) of the Cerrado’s native vegetation, which includes scrubland, grasslands and forests, is already used for agriculture,  the MapBiomas monitoring project calculates. And under Brazil’s far-right president, Jair Bolsonaro, who is supported by powerful farming lobbies, the rest of its 2m sq km is disappearing fast. Last year alone,  6,500 sq km was cleared – adding to the 283,000 sq km of forest, grassland and scrub cut down  since 2001.
  This year so far the Cerrado has seen more than  61,000 fire alerts – up from 39,000 in the whole of 2018, before Bolsonaro took power – raising fears for its future. It is an immense, important carbon sink, storing  hundreds of tons a hectare in its soil and deep root systems.
 Brazil’s Cerrado biome is a vast, tropical savannah stretching diagonally up through the middle of  Brazil that covers 2 million sq km, around 22% of the country, as well as parts of Bolivia and Paraguay.
 According to Brazil’s  environment ministry, the biodiversity-rich Cerrado has 11,620 plant, 1,200 fish and 837 fish species and its 200  mammals include jaguars, anteaters,  rhea birds and  tapirs. But over half of its landscape – grassland scrub and dry forest – has been  converted to agriculture as it produces soya for China, Europe and other markets. The region lost  105,000 square kilometres of native cover from 2008-2018, according to Reuters – 50% more than the Amazon, which has more legal protection.
 According to an  international study published by  Science magazine, around 27,000 properties in the Cerrado had carried out deforestation “in all likelihood illegally”, and 48% of properties were non-compliant with the requirements of Brazil’s forest code, such as protecting 20% of their land (compared to 80% in the Amazon). The study found around 20% of soya exports and 17% of beef exports from Brazil’s Amazon and Cerrado biomes to the European Union “may be contaminated with illegal deforestation”.
 Another  study by researchers from Dartmouth College in the US, published in Nature Sustainability magazine, found land clearing had changed the weather in the Cerrado. Temperatures were hotter during the corn growing season and evapotranspiration fell.
 Last year, in  an article published in Perspectives in Ecology and Conservation, scientists warned that “climate changes are likely to cause  local extinctions of several mammalian species throughout the  Cerrado biome”.
 “The Cerrado is like the water tank of Brazil. This is impacting the fluvial regime of rivers, of important rivers,” said Britaldo Soares-Filho, professor of environmental modelling at the Federal University of Minas Gerais. “The Cerrado is a biodiversity hotspot and is under a lot of pressure.”
 A diagonal swathe across the middle of Brazil, the Cerrado covers around 22% of the country. Its rich wildlife includes  11,620 plant species and more than 200 species of mammals, including jaguars, anteaters, rhea birds and tapirs.
  Ignored for years, the region took on more importance after the country’s capital was moved to Brasília in the middle of the Cerrado in 1960 and states in the centre-west began filling with farms. Brazil’s military dictatorship from 1964–1985 launched programmes to modernise Cerrado agriculture, improve its infrastructure and colonise its remoter areas.
 In the late 1970s, a Japanese-Brazilian programme was launched to help colonise and develop the region to guarantee soya supplies. Big multinational traders such as Cargill and Bunge moved in. Chinese investment helped  pave roads and improve ports. “They created a structure that favoured export,” said Roberto Miranda, a professor of sociology at the Federal University of Campina Grande.
 The Amazon – which is even larger than the Cerrado – was also being increasingly exploited. But that destruction was attracting international attention and concern: meanwhile the Cerrado stayed below the radar. In a 2017 paper, Soares-Filho and other researchers calculated that while 49% of the Amazon was in protected areas such as conservation units and indigenous reserves, just 7.7% of the Cerrado was. Under Brazil’s 2012 Forest Code, farmers are required to leave 80% of Amazon land as a “legal reserve”, but just 20% in most of the Cerrado region. “The Cerrado was the poor relation,” said Soares-Filho. As a result, agribusiness loved it.
 The region’s “agricultural frontier” is a huge area Matopiba, home to former president Dilma Rousseff’s Matopiba development agency. “In the agriculture area, we are still capable of picking low-hanging fruit – and at the same time, expanding the tree,”  Rousseff said at the launch in 2015. “That’s why it’s a very special moment.”
  Farmers who moved into the region were realistic. “Our ambition was never to get rich, but we wanted to have something in life,” said Paulo Dalto Neto, who came to farm here in 1997. Land was cheap in this dry, inhospitable region, but he has been able to improve his soil, develop the local roads and plant hardy soya strains with high yields.
  The losers were the Cerrado’s traditional communities, who usually did not have legal titles to land they had used for generations. Soya farmers prized this land and in some cases unscrupulous land dealers were able to buy or simply steal it by falsifying documents. “The soya here brought us very few jobs … and brought us many disadvantages,” said Juarez de Souza, 63, from the tiny  Melancias community in Piauí.
 De Souza’s community is now engaged in a legal battle to prove ownership of their land, while the river local people used for water and fishing has, according to De Souza, been polluted by pesticides. He says he has received threats from land grabbers looking for land to sell on to bigger farmers. “For them the soya is gold,” he said. “There is a lot of money.”
  Moreover, the change in land use is now altering the climate of the region. There is less rain and the temperatures are higher, he says: studies show deforestation contributes to  droughts and erratic river behaviour. And meanwhile the farmers chop away, bit by bit, at its natural vegetation.
  The problem is that, while Brazil struggles with an economic downturn as a result of the coronavirus pandemic, which has so far resulted in 169,000 deaths CHECK, the Cerrado’s production of soya, sold mostly for animal feed, is booming – and expected to reach a  record 135m tons for the 2020–2021 harvest. “This is the strength of our agribusiness,” Tereza Cristina Dias, Brazil’s agriculture minister, tweeted on  10 November. Brazil expects  record beef exports in November – with the lion’s share of the country’s cattle raised in Cerrado states.
 With Brazil running a heavy deficit, worsened by its emergency pandemic payments to tens of millions of people and a  record 14% unemployment, its booming exports of agricultural commodities such as soya and beef come as a relief, said Fábio Klein, a public accounts analyst at Tendências, a São Paulo consulting outfit. “Agriculture is a very competitive sector, and the income it generates, although concentrated, spills over the rest of the economy, which is good for the country and the government.”
  Brazilian soya’s high protein and oil count makes it especially popular in China, Brazil’s biggest soya customer, says Kory Melby, an American agribusiness consultant based in the Cerrado town of Goiânia. “International and domestic markets are telling Brazil that even though Brazil is expanding as fast as she can, we would like her to expand even faster,” he says. “That blows my mind.”
 The problem is that while the Cerrado may seem like low-hanging fruit, in the long term Brazil is losing yet more of its precious forests and wildlife. Their loss will come with a heavy cost.

title: The Cerrado：巴西至关重要的“水箱”如何从森林变成大豆田
title_en: The Cerrado: how Brazil’s vital ‘water tank’ went from forest to soy fields

note:
您可能不认识长期的游戏开发商徐若Jo，但您知道他的工作。在业界超过二十年的时间里，他创立了Studio Gigante（原始Xbox的Wrestlemania 21的制造商），并在诸如EA之类的行业巨头中度过了一段时间。他的荣誉包括托尼·霍克（Tony Hawk）的职业溜冰者（Pro Skater）与《搏击之夜》（Fight Night）所钟爱的特许经营权。
 尽管制作了这样的简历，但Tsui的第一爱好不是游戏手柄-他实际上是去学校学习电影的。而且在2010年代初期，“最近，我不想将其称为中年危机，但我意识到，在所有这段时间里我都没有拍过电影，”他最近对Ars说。
 Tsui做过一些视频工作-营销他从事的游戏；到处都是一些化妆短裤-从来没有一部故事片。幸运的是，当他在2012年与Polygon坐下来谈论自己的过去时，他意识到一个完美的主意一直在等待着他。在我们的独立游戏和《 King of Kong》现实中，他要做的只是看他的职业生涯：1993年，芝加哥，在即将到来的Mortal Kombat和NBA Jam背后的团队推动2D街机游戏中的像素化-传奇街机游戏开发商Midway Games。
 “回忆起来，“我们开始与Midway谈论我的历史，这激发了一个想法-很多人都知道这些游戏，但他们不知道背后发生了什么。” “考虑到这一点，好吧，我在90年代中期的Midway经历了一个不可思议的时代，那时一切都炸了。
  他继续说道：“这似乎是一个完美的主题：我非常了解这个主题，我知道与谁交谈，知道骨骼是什么，从那时起我仍然与每个人都有很好的关系。” “坦率地说，令我惊讶的是，总体上没有更多关于Midway的讨论，对工作室的整体看法以及所有这些游戏如何相互交流。我想，‘如果我不这样做，那别人就会去做。’”
 Tsui的处女作《插入硬币》（Insert Coin）历经5年多的工作，诱人的Kickstarter运动以及后来保存的存档镜头的宝库，今年春天在South by Southwest赢得了全球首映，在这种情况下，这意味着它仅存在于西南筛选器表格。 Tsui和他的团队仍然希望今年秋天可以参加一些节日，但是他不希望自己的首场纪录片停留很长时间。他说：“我想尽快将其推出。” “我只是想以正确的方式来做。”
    因此，现在就保存您的宿舍。每当插入硬币最终在观众面前出现时，您都希望跳入La Bomba并尽可能快地瓦解汽油，以完成这次旅行。
对于任何有幸最终赶上Insert Coin的人来说，Tsui的电影制作背景都是显而易见的。首次使用功能总监（和编辑）做出了一些明智的决定，使该文档的制作超出了标准的制作或幕后花销。
 首先开始：除了一个著名的Ed Boon漏洞，Insert Coin受访者的名单似乎还包括所有人。这部电影的制作期长，似乎花了很多时间。 Tsui经常坦率地在相机上显示行业偶像，其中包括程序员Eugene Jarvis（美国Cruis'n美国）和Mark Turmell（NBA Jam），设计师John Tobias（Mortal Kombat）以及像Midway首席执行官Neil Nicastro这样的企业重量级人物。 （感觉就像在一部电影中想要的每个街机战争故事一样。）
 但是，Insert Coin并不会止步于此。Tsui告诉Ars，他采访的人数可能是影片中最终人数的两倍。他追踪了那个时代的游戏记者，以描绘更广阔的视野。他采访了著名的游戏爱好者（从Ready Player One的作者Ernest Cline到游戏学者/作家Carly Kocurek），作为这些游戏的数百万粉丝的伪替身。甚至最初将约翰尼·凯奇（Johnny Cage）描绘成Midway版本的动作捕捉的武术专业人士丹尼尔·佩西纳（Daniel Pesina）也会出现。无论您是开发人员，专业游戏玩家或游戏记者，还是仅仅是Smash TV的粉丝，Insert Coin中的任何人都可以直接说出您使用这些游戏的经历。
 “很多看过电影的人都说感觉就像坐在酒吧里和某人聊天，”崔说。 “实际上，这就是我试图进行采访的方式：我是一个单人射击队，与我认识的人一对一地交谈。我想让它尽可能贴心。”
 尽管其董事对材料非常熟悉，但徐刚从字面上开始了在Midway游戏开发的职业生涯，并在公司飞涨的时候在那里度过了十年的时光。InsertCoin也毫不费力。 Tsui展示了Midway的一些失误和公司违规的例子，以及公司的许多里程碑式成就。换句话说，Insert Coin的观众将学到与Grid和Midway管理有关的财务知识，以及他们对Narc以及Terminator 2如何开创好莱坞灵感视频游戏的了解。
     “我希望人们知道事情发生的原因，而不仅仅是制作一个可以在DVD上找到的真实故事或幕后故事，” Tsui说。 “我希望人们知道像Narc这样的游戏对继续前进的游戏开发商产生了影响。而且，如果人们不了解游戏，我希望它们与他们的个性和所采取的行动有关。因为他们都是人物，所以您有Eugene Jarvis之类的人物，这是一个天才认证的疯子。您忍不住要接受一次精彩的采访。”
 但是影片的最大力量（最终使插入硬币能够赢得休闲街机玩家和对真人快打世界有百科全书知识的人的青睐）在于它的可访问性。 Tsui只是有很多东西可以巧妙地发放和利用。您会看到与Aerosmith进行的Revolution X动作捕捉会议，了解James Cameron赢得并用餐的情况，并沉浸在NBA Jam中出现的Mortal Kombat角色的镜头，然后NBA办公室取消了这个想法。 Tsui一次又一次地专业地展示了具有前瞻性的Midway是如何被证明的，因为公司的工作回顾起来就像今天许多常见游戏惯例的先驱：社交评论（Smash TV和消费主义），DLC（请参阅Smash TV的Pleasure Dome），电子竞技游戏（The Grid）或微交易（终结者2上的内斯特·克莱恩（Ernest Cline），游戏：“到终点并击败T-1000时，您已经花了18倍的时间看电影”）。
如今，访问所有这些镜头可能感到司空见惯-如果您要制作一部有关任何现代游戏，电视或电影实体的纪录片，则其所有镜头通常都经过预先数字化处理并正确存储了-但90年代初是一个不同的时代和街机柜的格式不同。对于粉丝和游戏历史学家而言，将所有这些功能整合到一个地方是一项了不起的服务，而Insert Coin则由于Tsui的忙碌和一点运气的结合而将其撤下。
  “很多时候，[纪录片]电影都是根据可用的材料制作的，但是作为首次制片人，没有人告诉我这一点，” Tsui说。 “所以我感到非常幸运，我从过去得到了很多资料，但是我的前任老板Ken Fedesna [Midway副总裁]几乎在他的办公室里制作过几乎所有视频。因为否则，在Midway破产并由Warner Bros.接管之后，他们将把这些材料从字面上扔掉。”
 Tsui大量利用了那个档案。在Fedesna去年捐赠了他的Midway藏品之后，它现在存在于纽约罗切斯特的Strong游戏博物馆（世界电子游戏名人堂和国际电子游戏历史中心的所在地）。作为庆祝费德斯纳（Fedesna）的庆祝活动的一部分，徐要求放映早期影片。如您所料，电影制片人记得这是一个很棒的夜晚，这是他首次在测试环节以外的大屏幕上展示插入硬币的机会。但是回想起来，如果插入硬币是那种可以通过视频游戏历史学家的嗅觉测试的游戏纪录片，那可能表示观众需要知道一切。希望这部电影在2020年首次公开放映之前不会太久，因此世界其他地方可以亲眼目睹。
note_en:
You may not know longtime game developer Joshua Tsui, but you know his work. Over two decades-plus in the industry, he founded Studio Gigante (makers of  Wrestlemania 21 for the original Xbox) and spent time at industry stalwarts like EA. His credits include beloved franchises from  Tony Hawk’s Pro Skater to  Fight Night.
 Despite building that kind of resume, however, Tsui’s first passion wasn’t the gamepad—he actually went to school to study film. And in the early 2010s, well, “I don’t want to call it a midlife crisis, but I realized after all this time I hadn’t made a film,” he told Ars recently.
 Tsui had done some video work—marketing for the games he worked on; a few making-of shorts here and there—just never a feature film. Luckily, when he sat down with  Polygon in 2012 to talk about his past, he realized the perfect idea had been waiting for him all along. In our post-  Indie Game and  King of Kong reality, all he had to do was look to his professional beginning: Chicago, 1993, pushing pixels on 2D arcade games with the teams behind   Mortal Kombat and   NBA Jam at the soon-to-be-legendary arcade developer, Midway Games.
 “We started talking about my history with Midway, and it sparked a bit of an idea—a lot of people know these games, but they don’t know what happened behind them,” Tsui recalls. “When I thought about it, well, I lived through this incredible era in the mid-‘90s at Midway when everything was blowing up.
  “It seemed like the perfect subject: I knew the subject really well, I knew who to talk to, I knew what the skeletons were, and I still had a really good relationship with everyone from back then,” he continued. “Frankly, I was surprised there hadn’t been more discussion about Midway in general, a holistic view of the studio and how all these games informed each other. I thought, ‘If I don’t do that, someone else is going to.’”
 Five-plus years of work, a tantalizing  Kickstarter campaign, and a treasure trove of saved archival footage later, Tsui’s debut feature film,  Insert Coin, earned a world premiere this spring at  South by Southwest—which in this case means it exists solely in screener form for now. Tsui and his team remain hopeful it can play some festivals this fall, but he doesn’t want his debut documentary stuck on the circuit for a super long time. “I want to get it out there as soon as possible,” he says. “I just want to do it the right way.”
    So, save up your quarters now. Whenever  Insert Coin finally gets in front of audiences, you’ll want to hop in your  La Bomba and smash the gas as fast as possible to take this trip down memory lane.
     Tsui’s filmmaking background will be obvious to anyone lucky enough to eventually catch  Insert Coin. The first-time feature director (and editor) makes several smart decisions that elevate this doc beyond standard making-of or behind-the-scenes fare.
 To start: beyond one notable Ed Boon-sized hole, the list of  Insert Coin interviewees seems to include  everybody. The film’s long production period seems to be time well spent. Tsui has industry icons on camera frequently and frankly, including programmers Eugene Jarvis ( Cruis'n USA) and Mark Turmell ( NBA Jam), designer John Tobias ( Mortal Kombat), and corporate heavyweights like Midway CEO Neil Nicastro. (It might feel like every arcade  War Story you could want in a single film.)
 But  Insert Coin smartly doesn’t stop there—Tsui tells Ars he probably interviewed twice the amount of people who ended up in the film. He tracked down game journalists from the era to paint the broader perspective. He interviews notable game enthusiasts (from   Ready Player One author Ernest Cline to  gaming academic/author Carly Kocurek) as pseudo stand-ins for the millions of fans of these games. Even Daniel Pesina, the martial arts pro who originally portrayed Johnny Cage for Midway’s version of motion capture, shows up. No matter if you’re a developer, a professional gamer or games journalist, or simply a fan of  Smash TV, someone in  Insert Coin speaks directly to your experience with these titles.
 “A lot of people who see the film have said it feels like sitting at the bar talking with someone,” Tsui says. “That’s actually how I tried to do the interviews: me as a one-person shooting team, talking one on one with the people I know. I wanted to make it as intimate as possible.”
 Despite its director being so intimately familiar with the material—again, Tsui literally started his career in game development at Midway and spent a half-decade there as the company soared— Insert Coin doesn’t pull punches, either. Tsui showcases some of Midway’s misses and instances of corporate transgressions right alongside the company’s many landmark achievements. To put it another way,  Insert Coin viewers will learn as much about  The Grid and Midway management financially pressuring creators as they will about  Narc and how  Terminator 2 pioneered Hollywood-inspired video games.
     “I wanted people to know why things happened, not to just make a straight making-of or behind-the-scenes story you can find on a DVD,” Tsui says. “I wanted people to know that a game like  Narc influenced the game developers moving on. And if people don’t know the games, I wanted them to relate to the personalities and the actions they took. Because they’re all characters—you have people like Eugene Jarvis, a certified genius and a mad man. You can’t help but get a great interview.”
 But the film’s greatest strength (and ultimately the thing that will allow  Insert Coin to win over both the casual arcade player and those with encyclopedic knowledge of the  Mortal Kombat universe) lies in its access. Tsui simply has  so much stuff to cleverly dole out and leverage. You see motion-capture sessions with Aerosmith for  Revolution X, hear about James Cameron wining and dining the team, and soak in footage of  Mortal Kombat characters appearing in  NBA Jam before the NBA offices nixed the idea. Again and again, Tsui expertly shows how forward-thinking Midway proved to be as the company’s work looks in retrospect like a precursor to many common gaming practices today: social commentary ( Smash TV and consumerism), DLCs (see  Smash TV’s Pleasure Dome), esport-friendly games ( The Grid), or microtransactions (Ernest Cline on  Terminator 2, the game: “By the time you reached the end and defeated the T-1000, you had spent enough to watch the movie like 18 times”).
 Accessing all of this footage might feel commonplace nowadays—if you’re making a documentary about any kind of modern game or TV or film entity, all its footage is usually pre-digitized and properly stored—but the early ’90s were a different time and arcade cabinets a different format. Getting all this together in one place is a tremendous service for fans and  gaming historians alike, and  Insert Coin pulled it off thanks to a combination of Tsui’s hustle and a little luck.
  “A lot of times, [documentary] films get made based on what material is available—but as a first-time filmmaker, nobody told me that,” Tsui says. “So I got very fortunate—I have a lot of materials from my old days, but my old boss, Ken Fedesna [VP at Midway], literally had almost every single video ever created at Midway in his office. Because otherwise, after Midway went bankrupt and Warner Bros. took over, they were going to literally toss these materials.”
 Tsui drew upon that archive heavily. It now exists at the Strong Museum of Play in Rochester, New York—home to the World Video Game Hall of Fame and the International Center for the History of Electronic Games—after Fedesna  donated his Midway collection last year. And as part of the festivities honoring Fedesna, Tsui was asked to show an early cut of his film. As you’d expect, the filmmaker recalls it being a great evening, his first chance to show  Insert Coin on the big screen outside of test sessions. But in retrospect, if  Insert Coin is the kind of game documentary that can pass the sniff test for that collection of video game historians, that likely says everything audiences need to know about what’s to come. Hopefully it won’t be too long until the film’s first public screenings of 2020 so the rest of the world can see for itself.
title: 插入街机纪录片Coin，值得您投入所有时间
title_en: Insert Coin, the arcade documentary worth feeding all your quarters into

note:
韩国个人信息保护委员会（PIPC）对Facebook处以67亿韩元（约合610万美元）的罚款，以在未经允许的情况下共享用户信息。韩国通讯委员会于2018年启动了调查，最终于几个月前将调查移交给了PIPC。根据Yonhap News的报道，PIPC确定该社交网络在2012年5月至2018年6月未经同意就与其他公司共享了330万韩国用户（总计1800万）的个人信息。
 监管机构说，Facebook在登录时与其他公司分享了人们的姓名，地址，出生日期，工作经历，家乡和亲戚关系。此外，Facebook还可以与多达10,000家其他公司共享该信息。也就是说，它无法说出确切共享了多少信息，因为Facebook显然没有提供相关文档。 PIPC还指控该社交网络提交了不完整或虚假的文件。 Facebook否认了这一指控，在一份声明中告诉《联合通讯》（Yonhap News）“它与整个调查合作”。
  这是PIPC首次对社交网络进行罚款，但这并不是Facebook首次在该国陷入困境。早在2018年，韩国通信委员会就因拖慢用户在2016年和2017年的连接而对该公司处以3.96亿韩元（约合396,706美元）的罚款。尽管如此，Facebook赢得了对该委员会的法律诉讼，但最终被取消。首尔行政法院当时裁定罚款是非法的，因为该公司无意减慢其在该国的服务。
note_en:
South Korea’s Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC) has slapped  Facebook with a 6.7 billion won (around US$6.1 million) fine for sharing user information without consent. The Korea Communications Commission kickstarted the investigation in 2018 before ultimately handing it off to the PIPC a few months ago. According to   Yonhap News, the PIPC determined that the social network shared the personal information of 3.3 million South Korean users (out of a total of 18 million) to other companies without consent from May 2012 to June 2018.
 The watchdog said Facebook shared people’s names, addresses, dates of birth, work experience, hometowns and relationship statuses with other companies when they logged in. Further, it said Facebook could have shared that information with up to 10,000 other companies. That said, it couldn’t say how much information was shared exactly, because Facebook apparently didn’t provide relevant documentation. PIPC also accused the social network of submitting incomplete or false documents. Facebook denied the accusation, telling  Yonhap News in a statement that it “cooperated with the investigation in its entirety.”
  This is the first time the PIPC has fined the social network, but it’s not the first time Facebook has been in trouble in the country. Back in 2018, the Korea Communications Commission  fined the company 396 million won (approximately $396,706) for slowing users' connections in 2016 and 2017. Facebook won the legal battle against the commission, though, and the fine was ultimately rescinded. The Seoul Administrative Court  ruled back then that the fine was unlawful, as the company didn’t intend to slow down its services in the country.
title: Facebook因未经许可共享用户数据而在韩国被罚款610万美元
title_en: Facebook fined $6.1 million in South Korea for sharing user data without consent
